question: Across studies on ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, and their oxidation products, what range of melting points has been reported for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and oxides?
answer:
The range of melting points reported for the ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and their oxidation products across the studies is as follows:

- ZrB2 has a melting point of 3245 ◦ C [6].
- HfB2 has a melting point of 3380 ◦ C [18].
- ZrC has a melting point of 3540 ◦ C [20].
- HfC has a melting point of 3890 ◦ C [1].
- The oxidation product of ZrB2, ZrO2, has a melting point of 2715 ◦ C [1].
- The oxidation product of HfB2, HfO2, has a melting point of 2810 ◦ C [1].
- The oxidation product of ZrC, ZrO2, has a melting point of 2715 ◦ C [1].
- The oxidation product of HfC, HfO2, has a melting point of 2810 ◦ C [1].

These values reflect the high-temperature stability of these materials and their oxides, which are crucial for their application in extreme environments.

refractory carbides, nitrides and borides of Hf, Zr and Ta, etc, which are classified as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), have remarkably raised owing to the striking properties, like supernal melting points, high excellent thermal conductivity, moderate coefficient of thermal expansion and high strength and modulus [4 e 7]. Refractory carbides including HfC (3890 /C14 C), TaC (3880 /C14 C) and ZrC (3540 /C14 C) with the highest melting points among UHTCs were reported to have lower vapor pressures than those of nitrides and borides, which were considered promising candidate materials for TPS [8 e 11]. The corresponding solid oxidation products, HfO2 (2810 /C14 C) and ZrO2 (2715 /C14 C) have higher melting points than that of Ta2O5 (1800 /C14 C), which could be more stable in environments above 2000 /C14 C [12]. However, the implementation of pure HfC and ZrC is restricted due to the weak oxidation inhibition at intermediate temperatures, forming porous oxide scale and making it susceptible to the ablative environment [13,14].

Introduction
Borides, carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals such as ZrB2, HfB2, HfC, or HfN belong to a class of ceramics defined ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with extremely high melting temperatures. These materials are of particular interest because of the unique property combination of high refractoriness, high-electrical and -thermal conductivity, chemical inertness against molten metals or nonbasic slags and good thermal shock resistance. 1,2 These properties make them attractive candidates for high-temperature applications where corrosion-wear-oxidation resistance are demanded. ZrB2 has several applications such as Hall-Heroult cell cathodes for electrochemical processing of aluminium, evaporation boats, crucibles for handling molten metals, thermowell tubes for steel refining, thermocouples sleeves for high-temperature uses, nozzles, plasma electrodes, or as dispersoid in metal and ceramic-matrix composites for heaters and igniters. In addition, ZrB 2 is a metallic conductor with electrical resistivity comparable with those of their parent metals. This permits to produce complex components at reduced shaping costs. HfB2, because of its higher costs of production, has found a limited number of applications, in wear-resistant coatings for instance. Similar to ZrB2, HfB2 has also been recently considered in ultra-high-temperature composites for aerospace applications (leading edge parts on re-entry vehicles), in conjuction with SiC, because of its excellent refractoriness. 3,4

1. Introduction
Refractory transition metal borides and carbides have extremely high melting points of over 3000 ◦ C and hence are referred to as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Even though they have been studied since the 1960s, there has been recent interest in these materials as potential candidates for thermal protection systems on hypersonic vehicles. A developmental history of UHTC materials can be found in Opeka et al. 1 and they have recently been reviewed by Paul et al. 2 The initial selection of UHTC materials was based on their melting temperatures, however oxidation temperature and the melting points of their oxides is, in fact, more critical. There are a number of materials with melting points over 3000 ◦ C, whose oxides also have melting points in excess of 2500 ◦ C, for example ZrB2, HfB2 and HfC. These materials are widely studied for high temperature

The desired properties may require the development of fibre reinforced UHTC composites to enable viable application development beyond 2500 ◦ C. For such hybrid materials, carbon fibre is the preferred choice owing to its high strength, ready availability and ability to be formed into complex shapes, 5 provided it can be protected from oxidation.

1. Introduction
Advanced materials offering a temperature capability significantly exceeding 2000 °C in extreme, highly corrosive environments are required for a range of aerospace and other applications where oxidation and/or erosion resistance is needed at very high temperatures [1]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are typically based on the borides, carbides and nitrides of refractory transition metals, including hafnium, zirconium and tantalum amongst others [1-3], not only offer extremely high melting points of over 3000 °C, but their oxides also typically have melting points over 2500 °C. Both hafnium and zirconium diborides, HfB2 and ZrB2, in particular have been extensively studied as innovative thermal protection systems (TPS) [4-6] and sharp leading edge components [7-9] for aerospace vehicles. They have typically been investigated as monolithic components; however they exhibit both poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance [10].
The use of fibre reinforcement can significantly improve the

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Even though borides are more extensively used for the ultra-high temperature applications, some carbides, such as the mono-carbides of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb, are also classified as UHTCs. Unlike the borides, these carbides crystallize in cubic close packed structures. The more commonly used HfC, ZrC and TaC possess face centered cubic or the NaCl structure (Wuchina, 2007; Pierson, 1996; Pierrat, 2010) (Figure 2a). Usually the host metal atoms form cubic close packed structures with

Compared  to  the  borides, still  higher  melting points have been reported for the carbides, with some solid solution carbides like Ta x Hf 1-x Cy possessing melting points in excess of 4000 o C (Cotton, 2010; Wuchina, 2007). However, unlike the borides, some issues related to their oxidation resistance (Wuchina, 2007) restrict their applications and this will
be discussed in the following section. In contrast to the M-B, the published M-C phase diagrams (Figure 2b) reveal presence of considerably wider homogeneity range, which implies presence of non-stoichiometry in their compositions and related defects in their lattices. Also, the presence of the defects such as carbon vacancies  allows  tailoring  of  the  thermo-physical  and  thermo-mechanical  properties (Wuchina, 2007; Pierrat, 2010; Wuchina, 2004).

4.1. Feasibility of EBS for UHTCs
Metal Boride UHTCs are unusual materials that possess ceramic-like properties such as high melting point, hardness, as well as metal-like properties such as high electrical and thermal conductivity. The UHTC material chosen for this research is ZrB2, which has an electrical and thermal conductivity of 1.08 × 10 7 S/m and 60 W/mK, respectively, and a melting temperature of 3245 ◦ C. With ZrSi2  as a sintering additive (melting point: 1625 ◦ C), it can be selectively melted, forming a liquid phase that binds the ZrB2  powder particles together, resulting in processing temperature around 1700 ◦ C that is easily attainable by the EBM machines.  Additionally,  ZrSi2  softens  around  800 ◦ C,  hence  a  liquid phase sintering process would most likely occur at temperatures less than 1700 ◦ C by an intergranular viscous ZrSi2 phase [11].
In the current study, a representative ZrB2 UHTC was mixed with 30 vol% of ZrSi2, which acts as a densification aid. It was determined by S. Guo  et  al.  [10]  that  in  the  traditional  UHTC  sintering  process,  the addition of ZrSi2  in the 30% volume fraction is critical in maintaining good  electrical  (11.9 × 10 6 S/m)  and  thermal  conductivities  (86.81 W/mK)  without  compromising  the  mechanical  behavior.  The  high electrical conductivity of the UHTC materials makes them compatible with the EBM process. The earlier work in our group [2] was the first of its  kind  to  demonstrate  the  feasibility  of  EBM  to  process  the  UHTC mixture  and  pioneer  EBS.  Although  only  1D  melt  tracks  have  been performed, interesting consolidation mechanisms have been reported. The current study will contribute more to the findings through experimentation and subsequent characterization of single-layer builds of the ZrB2 -30 vol% ZrSi2 powder feedstock.

C/C-ZrB2-ZrC-SiC Composites Derived from Polymeric Precursor Infiltration
UHTCs composites, especially diboride based UHTCs, such as ZrB 2 -SiC and HfB 2 -SiC have been studied intensively for this purpose because of their outstanding oxidation and ablation resistance at temperatures above 2000 ºC in a hypersonic oxidizing gas flow [see Ref. 11 and its cited references]. ZrB 2 exhibits  extremely  high melting point of 3245 °C with excellent high temperature mechanical properties, low cost and relative lower density (6.09 g/cm 3 ) compared to HfB 2 , which exhibits an even higher melting point, but with a rather higher density of 10.5 g/cm 3 and much expensive. During high temperature oxidation of ZrB 2 , it was found that a loose ZrO 2 solid layer and liquid B 2 O3 will be formed, the later phase will evaporates at temperatures higher than 1200°C, compared to the oxidation of ZrC in which only a porous ZrO 2 solid  is  left  (Chamberlain,

A B S T R A C T
In order to improve the ablation behavior in oxidizing atmospheres of carbon /uniFB01 ber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) and to increase their operation temperature, ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have been used as matrices. ' UHTCs ' is a general term for refractory metal diborides, carbides, and nitrides with a melting point of 2500 °C or more [15,16]. Since Zr-based (ZrB2, ZrC, and ZrN) and Hfbased (HfB2, HfC, and HfN) UHTCs produce oxides with a melting point of > 2500 °C, they are considered as potential CMC matrices. Some UHTCs form an oxide layer on their surface. This oxide layer covering their surface acts as a di /uniFB00 usion barrier of oxygen and shows oxidation resistance even above 2000 °C [15 -24].
Sayir has carried out pioneering research on carbon /uniFB01 ber-reinforced UHTC matrix composites (C/UHTCs) [25]. Carbon /uniFB01 ber-reinforced HfC composites (Cf/HfC) are a prototype of C/UHTCs. Various studies have been carried out to evaluate the applicability of C/UHTCs as aerospace heat-resistant materials. The development of C/UHTCs has accelerated over the past few years, and various studies have been carried out to investigate their microstructures, mechanical and/or thermal properties, and degradation (oxidation) behaviors. Herein, we have reviewed the studies on C/UHTCs, and have proposed some material design guidelines related to the fabrication process; microstructure; and mechanical, thermal, and ablative properties for the preparation of reliable C/UHTC composites.

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

Introduction
Important previous studies reported that MI of the Zr-Ti alloy is effective for forming ZrC and TiC dual phase matrices in porous carbon materials [25] and C/C preforms [26]. Zr-Ti alloys are all-proportional solid solutions, where the melting point of the alloys with Ti contents of 0-80 at% varies within the range of 1540-1700 /C176 C, which is higher than the melting point of Si and lower than the melting point of Zr [27]. The mechanical properties and heat resistance of Si-free Cf/UHTCMCs also overcome the heat resistance limitation determined by the evaporation temperature of B2O3 and SiO2. Previous studies showed that Zr-Ti alloy infiltration is effective for improving the heat resistance of C/UHTCMCs [26]. However, the applicability of these materials at extreme temperatures above 2000 /C176 C is unknown.
Thus, the objective of this study is to evaluate the potential utility of C/UHTCMCs with Zr-Ti alloy infiltration at temperatures of 2000 /C176 C or above. To evaluate the applicability of these materials, three types of C/UHTCMCs fabricated using different alloy compositions are subjected to arc-wind tunnel testing. The heat flux is set to below and above 2000 /C176 C. The microstructure is characterized, and the degradation behavior is examined based on thermodynamic analysis.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), integrated primarily by carbides, borides and some nitrides of early transition metals, are ultrarefractory materials that exhibit exceptional thermo-chemical stability and good mechanical strength even at high temperatures, which make them highly suitable for extreme environments [1 -3]. UHTCs find practical applications in aerospace [4], nuclear power, and hypersonic vehicles [5], where their excellent thermal and mechanical properties are convenient for the production of thermal shields, furnace linings, and structural components [6,7].
ZrB2 is the most studied UHTC, with a melting point of 3250 ◦ C [1], high strength and rigidity up to 2000 ◦ C [8], high hardness, remarkable chemical stability, and outstanding electrical and thermal conductivity [9 -13]. Despite its exceptional properties, pure ZrB2 faces several limitations for its practical industrial use. Due to its high melting point, it requires sintering temperatures ranging from 2100 to 2300 ◦ C, typically resulting in coarse microstructures with some residual porosity [14]. Additionally, pure ZrB2 exhibits brittle fracture and suffers from rapid oxidation at temperatures above 1127 ◦ C [15]. These weaknesses hinder its widespread adoption in high-temperature applications.

3.3.2. HfB2  and ZrB2  coatings
Boride  UHTC  coatings  are  mainly  composed  of  transition  metal diborides,  including  HfB2,  ZrB2,  TaB2 and  TiB2.  These  compounds exhibit metal-like and ceramic-like properties, such as a wide range of densities, high thermal conductivity, low resistivity, high melting point, high elastic modulus, hardness and good flexural strength. The unique combination of these properties renders them potential candidates for a variety of applications in high temperature and harsh environments [31, 104]. To date, HfB2 and ZrB2 have received the most extensive attention among boride UHTC coatings, mainly due to their ability to produce oxide phases with high melting point and a self-healing boride glass phase. This section focuses on the CVD-HfB2 and ZrB2 coatings, as well as their failure behaviors during high temperature applications.
HfB2 is  an  important  member  of  boride  UHTC  coatings.  As J. Zhang et al.
mentioned in Section 2.2, there are two kinds of deposition systems of HfB2: (i) the Hf(BH4)4 → HfB2 system cleaved from a single precursor; (ii) the HfCl4-BCl3-H2-Ar system using HfCl4  and BCl3  as precursor. Under the Hf(BH4)4 → HfB2  system, HfB2  films were grown by CVD using Hf [BH4]4 as a single precursor in ultra-low pressure (5 × 10 GLYPH<0> 9 Torr) and hydrogen-rich  sedimentary  environment.  The  obtained  films  under these conditions displayed high-purity nanostructures without halogen and organic pollutant. The mismatch between GaN and the HfB2  film was only 1.4 %, which can be used as a buffer layer on Si (111) substrate and applied in the field of microelectronics [154]. However, this method is difficult to be widely used in high temperature applications because of the thin coating thickness and the low deposition efficiency.

Thermal properties of La2O3-doped ZrB2and HfB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics
In a recent study, Jayaseelan et al. 21 showed the formation of a protective crystalline refractory coating formed during oxidation of UHTC as a consequence of the addition of some rare earth additives to SiC-reinforced ZrB2. In the particular case of La2O3 addition, the melting point of the crystal phase in the protective coating formed (La2Zr2O7) is ∼ 2200 ◦ C, which makes it a promising candidate for UHTCs needing to withstand temperatures around 2000 ◦ C. The goal of this work is the experimental study of thermal properties of ZrB2and HfB2-based ceramics at temperatures around 2000 ◦ C, including newly-developed La2O3-doped UHTCs to determine the validity of this approach in terms of thermal performance.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) refer to a class of transition metal borides, carbides, and nitrides with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C [1]. Owing to their exceptional high-temperature resistance, oxidation resistance, and thermal shock properties, they demonstrate significant  application  potential  in  extreme  environments  such  as aerospace, nuclear energy, and hypersonic vehicles, where they are used in critical components like rocket engine nozzles and thermal protection systems [2 -5]. Among these materials, the HfB2-SiC system stands out as a classic UHTC due to its high melting point, superior hardness, and excellent  high-temperature  performance  [6].  Extensive  research  has focused on enhancing its oxidation and ablation resistance. For instance, the incorporation of SiC into boride matrices promotes the formation of a protective SiO2  layer at elevated temperatures, improving oxidation resistance [7,8]. Monteverde et al. [9] fabricated HfB2-SiC composites via  hot  pressing,  demonstrating  that  SiC  addition  enhances  flexural strength  (770 ± 35  MPa)  and  hardness  (19  GPa)  while  boosting

3. Results and discussion
In order to ensure the accuracy of the selected network, we collected some other known experimental melting point data about several UHTC systems from the literature published, which were di /uniFB00 erent from the ones we used for training. The contents of each elements and corresponding experimental melting point data have been shown in Supplementary Table 1. Fig. 3 compares experimental melting points data and predicted melting points data. Obviously, the BPNN predicted the melting temperature of di /uniFB00 erent UHTCs systems precisely. The average absolute relative error (AARE) of the predicted results and the experimental results is as low as 5.09%, and R is 0.935. The darker green area in Fig. 3 indicates the relative errors of data points inside this area are less than 5%, and the lighter green area indicates the relative errors are less than 10%. It can be seen directly in Fig. 3 that most of the data points are in the darker area, which means the network we built can predict melting points of UHTCs exactly.
To give a more straightforward veri /uniFB01 cation of our model, the UHTCs melting temperature predicting model is applied on the Hf-C-N UHTCs system. Fig. 4(a) shows the liquids projection prediction of Hf-CN UHTCs system [24], where the relationship between contents and melting temperature has been shown directly. The red dashed line in Fig. 4(a) corresponds with Hf-xC-(1-x)N UHTCs system, for 0 < x < 1, and the yellow cycle suggests the highest predicted melting temperature. Much more details are shown in Fig. 4(b), that the highest melting temperature predicted is 4330 K with the corresponding composition of 0.76HfC·0.24HfN.

2. Crystal structure, bonding and phase diagram of transition metal borides
Both HfB and HfB2 phases have homogeneity range and HfB2 has the highest melting point (3380 °C) [57,59]. In Ta-B phase diagram, there are six boride phases, such as Ta2B, Ta3B2, TaB, Ta3B4, TaB2 and Ta5B6 [60]. Among these phases, only TaB and TaB2 are stable from room temperature (RT) up to their melting temperatures. The TaB2 phase, possessing a melting temperature of ~3200 °C, has a wide compositional range between 61 and 72 at.% B or 64 and 72 at.% B below the eutectic temperature of ~2055 °C [60]. The crystal structure, space group and lattice constants for the different phases of Ti-B, Zr-B, Hf-B and Ta-B binary systems have been presented in Table 2.

KEYWORDS
tive environments arising from ionic species generated from plasma. 1 Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) are unique materials meeting the above requirements. UHTCs such as ZrC, ZrB2, HfC, and HfB2 possess high melting points of 3532 ◦ C, 3246 ◦ C, 3900 ◦ C, and 3250 ◦ C, respectively, due to strong covalent bonding. UHTCs are the only materials of interest for sharp hot structures of hypersonic vehicles, such as leading edges and nose cones, where Mach numbers go beyond 6, resulting in surface temperatures in excess of 1800 ◦ C, solid rocket motors. 2,3 UHTCs possess high thermochemical stability, high thermooxidative stability, retention of mechanical properties at elevated temperatures, resistance to thermal shock, and good thermal conductivity. 4 The high stability of their oxides, that is, ZrO 2 with a melting point of 2715 ◦ C and HfO2 with a melting point 2758 ◦ C is the main reason for their high oxidation resistance in comparison to SiO 2 with a melting point of 1713 ◦ C. ZrB2 and ZrC and their composites are the most widely reported UHTCs in literature. 5 UHTCs used in combination with SiC at elevated temperatures will form a protective glassy phase of SiO 2 , SiOC, or SiBOC, which will melt and seal the surface pores, thereby avoiding further diffusion of oxygen into the bulk and enabling for use in extreme applications. 6 Hence, the present study focuses on synthesizing a mixed non-oxide UHTC system consisting of both ZrC and ZrB2 with SiC. Several routes are available for the synthesis of UHTC composite structures and the most reported one being the powder compaction of mixed powders of ZrC and SiC; and the other is coating ZrC particles with a SiCbased pre-ceramic polymer followed by hot pressing.

3.3.1. Zr-Based UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
ZrC is the most classic and commonly used single-phase UHTC, with low density (ZrC: 6.6 g/cm 3 , TaC: 14.5 g/cm 3 , HfC: 12.7 g/cm 3 ), thermal expansion coefficient, and cost, and it has outstanding ablation resistance, making it the first UHTC applied to modified C/C composites [25,41,42,51,84]. During ablation at temperatures higher than ca. 1800 ◦ C, ZrC can form relatively dense ZrO2, but when the ablation temperature is lower, the highmelting-point ZrO2 (melting point: 2700 ◦ C) forms a framework that cannot promptly compensate for defects (e.g., cracks and pores) caused by oxidation. The onset temperature for ZrC oxidation is around 800 ◦ C [85]. The oxidation of ZrC leads to significant volume change and CO/CO2 release, which cause lots of cracks and pores in the ZrO2 scale, respectively. When the oxidation temperatures are higher than ca. 800 ◦ C but lower than ca. 1800 ◦ C, the generated ZrO2 cannot be densified due to its high softening temperature. Therefore, no self-sealing effect can be obtained in this temperature range. In addition, the thermal expansion coefficient mismatch between the ZrC matrix and ZrO2 scale also results in cracks at high temperatures. Under this condition, these defects are the oxygen diffusion channels, leading to a decrease in protective capability. Liu et al. [69] used ICVI combined with RMI to prepare C/C-ZrC composites. After 60 s of oxyacetylene ablation, the mass ablation rate was 0.422 mg/s/cm 2 , attributed to the formation of relatively dense ZrO2, which reduced the oxidation of PyC and carbon fibers. 2.1.1. Carbide UHTCMCs of Cf/UHTC type
A signi /uniFB01 cant number of researchers concentrated their efforts on investigating UHTCMCs with a ZrC matrix (Table 1) because of its high melting point (3540 /C14 C [92,94]), reduced fabrication cost and density in comparison to HfC- and TaC-based ceramics [40,96], and its excellent ablation resistance [97 e 99]. According to Ref. [96], Cf/ZrC exhibits excellent con /uniFB01 gurational stability with surface temperatures above 2000 /C14 C for 60 e 300 s, while the 3D Cf/SiC composite perforates in 55 s. Two different regions can be identi/uniFB01 ed on the ablated Cf/ZrC surface: a melting region formed from a viscous ZrO2 melt and a porous region, which is a loose ZrO2 layer with lots of /uniFB01 ne pores [70,96,100 e 102]. In addition, studies have identi /uniFB01 ed the presence of an undersurface oxidized layer [70,96] or the oxygen diffusion layer [102]. The authors [96] suggested that the ablation process could be divided into two steps. The /uniFB01 rst step was the oxidation of the carbon /uniFB01 ber and ZrC matrix at a relatively low temperature, resulting in the formation of a porous structure. The second step was the formation of ZrO2 melt while the temperature increase above 2800 /C14 C. At the initial stage of ablation, the porous ZrO2 layer was unable to protect the inner substrate from oxidation at temperatures below the melting point, allowing oxidant to easily pass through it [42]. A growth of the oxide /uniFB01 lm wasobserved. The formation of the porous ZrO2 layer was the result of reactions between ZrC and C with O2 [70]:

3.4. Multiphase UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
For example, the melting point of metallic Zr is 1855 ◦ C, while Zr-Si can form two eutectic alloys with lower melting points-Zr9Si1 (melting point: 1570 ◦ C) and Zr1Si9 (melting point: 1370 ◦ C)-allowing for RMI processes at lower temperatures; (2) the thermal expansion coefficients of SiC are close to those of C/C, significantly lower than those of UHTCs and their oxides, preventing micro-cracks or damage to carbon fibers during composite preparation; (3) during ablation, SiO2 exhibits excellent fluidity, which helps compensate for defects caused by oxidation of UHTCs, addressing issues of oxide cracking and delamination from the matrix; and (4) the formation of SiO2-UHTCs oxide symbiotic structures or silicates further resists the erosion of flame jets and oxygen penetration, thus better protecting the C/C matrix [71,121].

3.3.4. Ti-Based UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
TiC is a commonly used UHTC in lightweight design, with a density of 4.9 g/cm 3 , significantly lower than ZrC (6.6 g/cm 3 ) and HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ). Its melting point is 3100 ◦ C, and its vapor pressure is relatively low (~10 -7 atm at 1800 ◦ C), allowing it to maintain structural stability at high temperatures [103]. During ablation, the TiO2 ceramic phase formed from TiC oxidation has a high viscosity and melting point (1843 ◦ C), forming a continuous and dense protective film. It exhibits some fluidity in the mid-temperature range (1000-1600 ◦ C) and can dynamically repair ablation defects. In the temperature range of 2000-2400 ◦ C, it shows excellent erosion resistance. TiC is often used in conjunction with SiC or with other UHTCs by forming solid solutions [23,81,104,105], while C/C-TiC composites are less common. Although there are some related patents, most focus on its mechanical properties rather than its ablation resistance [103].

2.4. UHTC efficacy
Application  to  hypersonics  is  based  on  protective  oxidation  and melting points of oxidation and reaction products, barring all but HfC based materials for this type of hypersonic utilization at 2000 ◦ C and above. What is not indicated in Fig. 2 are the relative amounts of the reaction products formed. It may be possible that a judicious amount of Ta2O5 can be formed during HfC-TaC oxidation to facilitate liquid phase sintering of oxide scales, leading to a rate limiting interlayer with even higher density.
Of  the  multitude  of  reported  MAX-phases,  only  Al4SiC4  currently shows any promise for UHTC application (Table 1). During oxidation, silica, alumina and mullite form [50]. The impact of differences in melt viscosity,  (which  are  discussed  in  a  later  section,  Fig.  18),  needs consideration. For high-entropy ceramics (HECs) [51 -53], which are the newest  class  of  ceramics  presently  discussed,  the  sparse  oxidation studies indicate selective oxidation of group IV elements may deplete the underlying ceramic, potentially nullify the high entropy effect. There is not enough data to currently qualify this emerging class of material. Rare-earth modified Zr/HfB2-SiC composites show initial promise due to refractory  zirconate  formation  [54,55],  but  there  is  a  lack  of  higher temperature oxidation studies to conclude on these materials.
In the following sections, the specifics of oxidation mechanisms, like kinetics,  reaction  products,  and  protective  regimes,  are  discussed  in detail.  The  materials  are  covered  generally  in  order  of  number  of available studies and also loosely in chronological order, beginning with monolithic diborides, and ending with HECs. It ' s strongly encouraged to read each material ' s section prior to discreditation, as evaluation can be more nuanced than Figs. 2 and 3, and Table 1 may suggest.

1. Introduction
Generally,  Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  refer  to  ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ C, including carbides, nitrides and borides of transition metals such as Zr, Hf, and Ta [1 -3]. Known for their exceptionally high melting points, hardness, modulus and excellent high-temperature oxidation resistance, UHTCs have garnered significant attention in thermal protection applications [4]. Among them, HfC and TaC are extensively studied [5]. Ta and Hf, located in groups IVB and VB of  the  periodic  table,  have  similar  atomic  radii  due  to  lanthanide contraction, enabling unlimited mutual solubility to form Ta-Hf-C solid solutions [6]. Ta4HfC5  synthesized in a molar ratio of 4:1 of TaC:HfC exhibits  a  melting  point  approaching  4000 ◦ C  [7].  Due  to  the  high melting  point  and  superior  thermophysical  and  oxidation-resistant properties inherited from TaC and HfC, Ta4HfC5  holds promising applications in extreme high-temperature aerospace environments [8 -10].
To apply Ta4HfC5 in thermal protection materials, two fundamental

KEYWORDS
Developing novel ultra-high-melting-point UHTCs with enhanced elevated-temperature mechanical performance and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance is imperative to address the critical thermal barrier in hypersonic vehicle TPS. 7,8 Hafnium carbonitride (HfCN) ceramics, with a melting point of up to 4419 ± 37 K, have become the material with the highest melting point among all discovered compounds, and have gradually received high attention in TPS in recent years. 9-11 Suvorova et al. 11 synthesized HfCN ceramics via spark plasma sintering (SPS), followed by evaluation of their mechanical properties and oxidation resistance at 1200 ◦ C. Zeng et al. 12 synthesized HfCN ceramic powder via hafnium nitride carbonization and consolidated it by SPS, demonstrating superior ablation resistance over commercial HfC. Meanwhile, Peng et al. 13 investigated the ablation/oxidation behavior of HfCN ceramics under ultra-high temperatures (3273 K), revealing a 90% reduction in linear ablation rate compared to monolithic HfC ceramics. In addition, Xu et al. 14 fabricated HfCN via hot-pressing sintering, assessing its thermomechanical properties from RT-2000 ◦ C under Ar and revealing significant C/N ratio dependence of mechanical properties in HfC x N 1-x ceramics. The existing studies have preliminarily shown that HfCN ceramics possess significant potential for application in TPS of hypersonic vehicles. However, most high-temperature mechanical properties research on HfCN materials has focused on argon or vacuum environments, and the hightemperature mechanical properties of HfCN ceramics in air environments lack systematic investigation, which is critical to the safety and application in TPS. Furthermore, current fabrication restricts bulk HfCN ceramics to diameters below 50 mm. Addressing these gaps requires, there is a pressing demand for the development of large-scale HfCN ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
Carbide ceramics show the highest known melting point in UHTCs and are widely investigated as thermal protection structures for hypersonic vehicles [2,13]. Meanwhile, ZrC or HfC based UHTCs show effective ablation resistance due to the high melting point and low vapor pressure of their oxide products [14 -16]. However, the high density of Hf-based components [17] and the porous oxide product ZrO2 generated by ZrC [18] limit their further application. TiC is often added to other UHTCs [19,20] because its oxidation product, TiO2, is a stable oxide with a low oxygen diffusion coefficient (1.12 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 s GLYPH<0> 1 ) [21]. In addition, ZrC, HfC, and TiC have the same crystal structure, i.e., rock salt structure, and Zr, Hf, and Ti atoms have similar atomic radii, which can be solid solved. So, a MC-UHTC, i.e., (Zr,Hf,Ti)C has attracted attention. Lun et al. [22] found that non-stoichiometric ratio of (Zr,Hf,Ti)C ceramic powders have better oxidation performance than the binary ceramic powders. In addition, (Zr1/3Hf1/3Ti1/3)C ceramics with equal atomic ratios of transition metal elements possess higher entropy values in (Zr,Hf,Ti)C [23,24].
In this study, (Zr1/3Hf1/3Ti1/3)C ceramics were prepared by hotpressed sintering, and non-isothermal oxidation experiments, isothermal oxidation experiments under different oxidizing conditions and ablation experiments were carried out on the (Zr1/3Hf1/3Ti1/3)C ceramics to investigate the role of each principal element in the oxidation and ablation process, and to analyze the oxidation and ablation properties.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), particularly HfC, effectively protect aerospace vehicles in extreme environments [1,2]. However, the high melting point (T m ≈ 2810 ◦ C [3]) and phase-transformation stress [4] of HfO2 result in pulverised oxidation of HfC  coating  [5,6],  limiting  its  ability  to  inhibit  oxygen  diffusion. Component modification of HfC coatings has been extensively studied. For instance, under doping with SiC [7], SiO2 evaporates at 1650 ◦ C [8] and SiO sublimates at 1710 ◦ C [9], resulting in a porous oxide film. Introduction of TaC [7], the low T m of Ta2O5 [10] leads to a discontinuous  oxide  film.  For  HfO2 [11],  although  the  formation  of  gaseous products is suppressed, the porous structure of the HfO2 layer does not significantly densify. Furthermore, Y2Hf2O7 coating [12] forms a dense oxide film under an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 ; however, Y 3 + increases the concentration of oxygen vacancies [13]. To date, no public reports have documented the formation of a dense single-phase HfO2 film on the surface of HfC coating after ablation.
NbC oxidises into a liquid state at 1500 º C in air [14] and produces a dense  filmy  structure  under  an  oxyacetylene  flame  in  the  range  of 2000 -2100 º C [15],  indicating  its  excellent  wide-temperature  range

3.3.1. HfC, ZrC and TaC coatings
As mentioned in Section 3.1, the melting point of HfC is as high as 3890 ◦ C, and that of its oxidation product (HfO2) is reached 2810 ◦ C. Notably, HfC exhibits no solid phase transition during heating or cooling, resulting in superior thermal shock resistance, which is the main research focus as one of the key HTA coating components [27]. In the early stage, Emig and Ache et al. [97,133] prepared HfC coatings on the surface of carbon fibers and C/SiC composites using HfCl4, CH4 and H2 precursors with a CVD reactor and used thermodynamic calculations to predict  the  optimal  preparation  temperature  of  HfC.  The  established thermodynamic model demonstrated the possibility of fabricating HfC layer without excess carbon, while also revealing the limited oxidation resistance of HfC above 600 ◦ C [134]. Although CVD-HfC coating have unique  advantages,  the  preparation  of  high-quality  coating  requires exploring  the  influence  of  process  parameters  on  the  composition, microstructure and uniformity of coatings. Wang et al. [81,135] studied the effects of H2  concentration and C/Hf ratio on the microstructure, growth behavior and mechanical properties of HfC coating. With the increase of H2  concentration, the deposition efficiency of HfC coating gradually increased, and the microstructure of the coating changes from particle stacking to columnar crystals (Fig. 12(a)). The high concentrations of H2  inhibited the decomposition of CH4  and reduced the nucleation  of  the  coating  grain,  resulting  in  the  growth  behavior  of  HfC dominated by grain growth and the formation of columnar arrangement structure  with  exceptional  mechanical  properties  (Fig.  12(b)). 1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) typically refer to a type of ceramic material with a melting point above 3000 ℃ ,  including carbides, nitrides and borides of transition metals such as Zr, Hf, and Ta [1 -3]. Among them, TaC and HfC are two types that have received broad wide attention and research because of their advantages such as high melting  point,  high  hardness  and  high  modulus  [4 -6].  Due  to  the contraction of the lanthanide series, the atomic radii of Ta and Hf are very close, which means TaC and HfC can theoretically be infinitely soluble and can form a series of Ta-Hf-C solid solutions. According to the existing literature reports [7 -9], Ta4HfC5 synthesized with a molar ratio of 4:1 between TaC and HfC has a melting point close to 4000 ℃ , and also  possesses  excellent  thermal  and  physical  properties  as  well  as anti-oxidation and erosion resistance. Therefore, it has great potential for application in ultra-high temperature service environments [10].
As reported in the existing literature, the preparation methods of Ta4HfC5  include  direct  chemical  combination,  solid  phase  reaction,

question: What melting point values have been reported for ultra-high-temperature ceramics generally versus hafnium and zirconium diborides, carbides, and their oxides specifically?
answer:
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are generally defined as materials with melting points above 3000 °C [15]. Specifically, hafnium and zirconium diborides, carbides, and their oxides have the following melting points:

- HfC (hafnium carbide) has a melting point of 3900 °C [15].
- TaC (tantalum carbide) has a melting point of 3800 °C [15].
- ZrC (zirconium carbide) has a melting point of 3530 °C [20].
- HfB2 (hafnium diboride) has a melting point of 3380 °C [3].
- ZrB2 (zirconium diboride) has a melting point of 3245 °C [15].
- HfO2 (hafnia) has a melting point of 2758 °C [10].
- ZrO2 (zirconia) has a melting point of 2715 °C [14].

These values highlight the exceptionally high melting points of UHTCs, particularly for the carbides and diborides of hafnium and zirconium.

1. Introduction
Advanced materials offering a temperature capability significantly exceeding 2000 °C in extreme, highly corrosive environments are required for a range of aerospace and other applications where oxidation and/or erosion resistance is needed at very high temperatures [1]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are typically based on the borides, carbides and nitrides of refractory transition metals, including hafnium, zirconium and tantalum amongst others [1-3], not only offer extremely high melting points of over 3000 °C, but their oxides also typically have melting points over 2500 °C. Both hafnium and zirconium diborides, HfB2 and ZrB2, in particular have been extensively studied as innovative thermal protection systems (TPS) [4-6] and sharp leading edge components [7-9] for aerospace vehicles. They have typically been investigated as monolithic components; however they exhibit both poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance [10].
The use of fibre reinforcement can significantly improve the

Do we have a relation between melting point (Tm) and maximum service temperature (Tmax )? No doubt that under all conditions T m > Tmax. However, in general, one may expect that higher melting point leads to the higher maximum working temperature. For example, for metals and alloys it is a rule of thumb that T max ∼ 0.6-0.7T m . The melting point for TaC is 3768 ◦ Candmaximumservicetemperature in vacuum ∼ 3000 ◦ C, which suggest that T max /T m ∼ 0.8. Silicon carbide (SiC) does not melt but begins to sublimate near 2730 ◦ C, while its working temperature is ∼ 1650 ◦ C, that is Tmax /T m ∼ 0.6. Different types of carbon with sublimation points above 3000 ◦ C have a T max of approximately 2500 ◦ C. Therefore, if we define UHTCs as a class of materials capable of operating at temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C, their melting or sublimation points should be above 3000 ◦ C. With this in mind, and considering binary and ternary non-oxide ceramics, the list of UHTCs remains relatively short (see Table 1).

Both HfB and HfB2 phases have homogeneity range and HfB2 has the highest melting point (3380 °C) [57,59]. In Ta-B phase diagram, there are six boride phases, such as Ta2B, Ta3B2, TaB, Ta3B4, TaB2 and Ta5B6 [60]. Among these phases, only TaB and TaB2 are stable from room temperature (RT) up to their melting temperatures. The TaB2 phase, possessing a melting temperature of ~3200 °C, has a wide compositional range between 61 and 72 at.% B or 64 and 72 at.% B below the eutectic temperature of ~2055 °C [60]. 1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is an ultra high temperature ceramic (UHTC) possessing high thermomechanical strength and chemical resistance. With a melting point of ∼ 3200 ◦ C it is one of the leading candidates for thermal protection systems in hypersonic flight and refractory materials such as crucibles for high temperature metals processing [1,2]. However, its densification is challenging even by the use of pressure and pulsed electric current/spark plasma sintering (SPS) methods.
Densification of ZrB2 powders can be improved by additives. However, the selection of the additive for refractory applications can be crucial to thermomechanical strength. Higher melting point metals such as Ir, W, Nb, and Mo are often selected in up to

INTRODUCTION
With laser pulse heating (heating time about 1 s), the  authors  of  [4]  found  that  the  congruent  melting temperature of TaC Tm = 4277 ± 12 K is higher than the melting point of HfC Tm =  4250  ±  20  K.  From other studies [5-7] with rapid microsecond heating, it follows  that  the  most  refractory  is  mixed  carbide Ta0.8 Hf0.2 C with a melting point 4300 ± 80 K [7]. For HfC and TaC in [5, 6], the literature data on the radiative characteristics at temperatures below the melting point  were  used.  A  consequence  of  this  was  an increased  temperature  measurement  error,  approximately equal to the difference in the melting temperatures of the mentioned carbides.
In theoretical study [8], the question of substances with maximum melting temperatures was considered and it was predicted that the maximum melting temperature, exceeding all known ones, will be that of hafnium carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.38 . Review [9] provides a detailed analysis and comparison of the properties of HfC, HfN, and Hf(C,N); in accordance with [8], a conclusion is drawn that the carbonitride HfC 0.51 N0.38 may have a record high melting point 4321-4445 K. In stationary experiment [10], HfC carbide and hafnium  carbonitride  HfC 0.5 N0.35 were  heated  under identical  conditions,  which  yielded  the  melting  of HfC,  while  hafnium  carbonitride  remained  in  an unmelted state.
In this article, the physical properties of carbonitride HfC0.51 N0.32 are studied for the first time based on a fast pulse experiment (below, this carbonitride is denoted Hf(C,N)) at high temperatures in solid and liquid  states,  including  the  melting  region,  and  its melting point is estimated.

1. Introduction
Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are those with extremely high melting points that give them a potential advantage for use in thermal protection systems or refractory applications. In particular, zirconium and hafnium diborides and carbides have melting points in excess of 3000 ◦ C. 1 In addition, diborides have higher thermal conductivity than carbides which make them promising candidates in thermal protection systems for hypersonic applications. The combination of high temperature capability and high thermal conductivity is particularly beneficial. However, ZrB2 and HfB2 baseline UHTCs possess insufficient oxidation resistance for hypersonic flight environments, in which temperatures above 2000 ◦ C in highly oxidizing atmospheres are reached. Most research to date has focused on developing more oxidation-resistant UHTCs composites. 2-5 The best oxidation resistance has been found for UHTCs containing SiC, typically additions between 10 and

A B S T R A C T
In order to improve the ablation behavior in oxidizing atmospheres of carbon /uniFB01 ber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) and to increase their operation temperature, ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have been used as matrices. ' UHTCs ' is a general term for refractory metal diborides, carbides, and nitrides with a melting point of 2500 °C or more [15,16]. Since Zr-based (ZrB2, ZrC, and ZrN) and Hfbased (HfB2, HfC, and HfN) UHTCs produce oxides with a melting point of > 2500 °C, they are considered as potential CMC matrices. Some UHTCs form an oxide layer on their surface. This oxide layer covering their surface acts as a di /uniFB00 usion barrier of oxygen and shows oxidation resistance even above 2000 °C [15 -24].
Sayir has carried out pioneering research on carbon /uniFB01 ber-reinforced UHTC matrix composites (C/UHTCs) [25]. Carbon /uniFB01 ber-reinforced HfC composites (Cf/HfC) are a prototype of C/UHTCs. Various studies have been carried out to evaluate the applicability of C/UHTCs as aerospace heat-resistant materials. The development of C/UHTCs has accelerated over the past few years, and various studies have been carried out to investigate their microstructures, mechanical and/or thermal properties, and degradation (oxidation) behaviors. Herein, we have reviewed the studies on C/UHTCs, and have proposed some material design guidelines related to the fabrication process; microstructure; and mechanical, thermal, and ablative properties for the preparation of reliable C/UHTC composites.

1. Introduction
Hafnium diboride (HfB2) is a refractory metal boride that exhibits excellent  thermal  and  mechanical  properties,  including  high  melting point  (Tm~3380 ◦ C),  high  hardness  (~28  GPa),  and  good  oxidation resistance  [1 -4].  These  properties  make  it  a  promising  material  for extreme environments, such as protective tiles on nose cones and leading  edges  of  re-entry  vehicles.  Despite  the  promising  combination  of properties,  monolith  HfB2  has  limited  applications  owing  to  its  poor sinterability.  The  high  melting  point  and  strong  covalent  bonding  of HfB2 pose  challenges  to  its  sintering  process,  impeding  complete densification  and  leading  to  significant  grain  growth  during  conventional sintering (T > 0.5Tm) [3].
To address these challenges, various sintering techniques, such as hot pressing  (HP)  [1,5 -7],  pressure-less  sintering  (PS)  [8],  reactive  hot pressing (RHP) [9], self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [10], and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [11 -18], have been employed to enhance  the  densification  of  HfB2-based  composites.  Overall,  these studies demonstrate that SPS is an effective technique for consolidating HfB2 ceramics with high relative density (up to ~ 98 %) and homogeneous  microstructure  [3,4,15 -20]. The sintering temperature range for densification of ZrB2 is influenced by melting point of the silicides used as the sinter aids [34,134,136,137]. The densification temperature (1600 °C) is lower for ZrSi2-reinforced ZrB2 when compared to other silicides since the melting point of ZrSi2 is relatively low. Sciti et al. [112,134,149] extensively studied the use of MoSi2 sinter-additive for ZrB2/ HfB2. Their work revealed that achievement of near-theoretical sinter-density was possible for ZrB2 with the use of MoSi2 (3-20 vol %) via PS at 1900 °C, HP at 1800 °C and SPS at 1750 °C. The densification was assisted by transient liquid phase that formed due to the reaction between MoSi2 and surface oxide impurities on ZrB2, as was shown by Eq. (30). During hot pressing, the plastic deformation of silicides at high temperatures (> 800 °C) would also contributes to the densification of ZrB2 as the deformation forces soft silicide particles to fill in the voids left by ZrB2 particles [112,134].

Recently, ablation mechanisms of HfC -SiC modi /uniFB01 ed C/C composites were investigated [168]. 1. Introduction
Certain characteristics of ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) materials containing transition metal borides and carbides give them a potential advantage over more traditional materials for use in hypersonic applications. In particular, hafnium and zirconium diborides and carbides, as well as their oxides, hafnia and zirconia, have extremely high melting points, all in excess of 2500 ◦ C (4530 ◦ F). 1 While HfC and ZrC have higher melting points than HfB2 and ZrB2, the diborides have substantially higher thermal conductivities than the carbides. 2,3 This combination of high-temperature capability and high thermal conductivity makes HfB2 and ZrB2 particularly attractive for use in sharp wing leading edges (WLEs) and nose tips. 4 In practice, the high-temperature oxidation resistance of pure diboride materials is not sufficient for aerothermal flight environments. The best oxidation performance is found for monolithic materials hot-pressed from mixtures of ceramic powders containing a silica former as a minor component. 5,6 The most common compositions typically contain 10-30 vol.% SiC, 7-12 although other silica formers like MoSi2 and TaSi2 have also been investigated. 13-16
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +1 650 859 2667; fax: +1 650 859 6196. E-mail address: jochen.marschall@sri.com (J. Marschall).
The design of high-performance hypersonic vehicles generally involves relatively sharp nose tips and WLEs. (An arcjet model of a concept UHTC WLE section is shown in Fig. 1.) While not an achievable design in practice, in the context of supersonic flow theory, a 'sharp' leading edge is one with zero radius of curvature. In a practical design context, 'sharp' usually refers to a leading-edge radius that is much smaller than the nose, wing, or vehicle length scale, while 'blunt' implies the converse.

Ceramics International
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are conventionally defined as inorganic materials with melting temperatures at or above 3000 ◦ C. Chemically, they are identified as carbides, borides, and nitrides of group IV and V transition metals [11 -13]. Among the various compositions,  the  diborides  of  zirconium  (ZrB2)  and  hafnium  (HfB2) retain desirable physical, mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties in addition to oxide phases that are thermally stable above 2500 ◦ C [38]. The  superior  melting  temperatures  for  Zr-  and  Hf-based  oxides  are depicted in Fig. 1. Based on these characteristics, UHTCs have garnered wide attention as enabling TPS for hypersonic air vehicles, especially for sharp leading-edge geometries used to reduce drag. During hypersonic flight, shockwaves that are generated on the leading-edge surface induce a corrosive plasma layer and significant aerothermal heating (~2000 ◦ C)  [39].  UHTCs  are  expected  to  address  the  thermal  and oxidation requirements for these conditions. However, exposure under simulated test environments has shown that the low toughness of these M. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  have  gained  renewed attention  in  the  last  decades  due  to  their  potential  use  as  protective coatings on metal substrates in extreme environments [1 -5]. By definition, UHTCs are ceramic materials with melting points above 3000 ◦ C [6,7].  Therefore,  they  are  suitable  candidates  for  thermal  protection structures in gas-turbine engines or chemical plants, which can withstand  application  temperatures  exceeding  ~1600 ◦ C  and  aggressive environmental conditions [1,8 -11]. In addition to their high melting point,  UHTCs  combine  improved  structural  properties  such  as  high hardness,  excellent  chemical  stability,  high  Youngs  modulus,  high refractoriness, as well as high thermal and electrical conductivity [1, 8 -10,12]. UHTCs are typically binary compounds consisting of carbides, borides or nitrides of early transition metals (e.g. ZrB2, HfN, HfC or TaC) [7,8,12]. HfC and its solid solutions with TaC such as (Hf0.2Ta0.8)C are

1. Introduction
Hafnium  diboride  (HfB2)  has  a  hexagonal  crystal  structure  (MB2 type, P6/mmm space group) consisting of B atoms layers in 2D graphitic rings and hexagonal close-packed Hf layer [1], which endows HfB2 with high melting point (3250 ◦ C), excellent chemical stability, high electrical and thermal conductivity, good thermal shock resistance and so on [2 -4].  Therefore,  HfB2 can  be  used  as  one  of  the  most  promising ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  applied  in  some  extreme  working conditions, such as in hypersonic vehicles, rocket thrusters, and other thermal protection components [5 -9]. However, strong covalent bond and low self-diffusion coefficient of HfB2 make the densifying HfB2 very difficult [10]. In addition, the oxides (such as B2O3, HfO2) on the surface of HfB2  powder further hinder its densification sintering [11 -13]. As a consequence, some additives such as SiC [14,15], B4C [16,17], WC [18, 19], MoSi2 [20 -22], etc.) through reacting with HfO2 to reduce the onset temperature of densification are employed to densify the HfB2 powder by traditional hot pressing [23]. However, the introduction of impurities from the surplus additives and the reaction product is bound to affect the property  of  the  material.  Besides,  in  order  to  achieve  the  densifying HfB2, the long dwell time (1 -2h) at high temperature (above 2100 ◦ C) inevitably results in the grain coarsening from initial powder grain size

question: What volume-fraction ranges have been reported for sintering additives and silica-forming secondary phases in boride-based UHTC composites?
answer:
The volume-fraction ranges for sintering additives in boride-based UHTC composites have been reported as follows:

- For ZrSi2 as a sintering additive in ZrB2-based composites, a volume fraction of 30 vol% was used [1].
- For MoSi2 or TaSi2 as sintering additives in boride-based composites, volume fractions of 15 vol% were used [2].
- For SiC as a sintering additive in ZrB2-based composites, volume fractions ranging from 10 to 20 vol% were used [3].
- For MeSi2 (such as MoSi2, HfSi2, or WSi2) as sintering additives in ZrB2-based composites, a volume fraction of 5 vol% was used [4].

The sources do not contain specific volume-fraction ranges for silica-forming secondary phases in boride-based UHTC composites.

4.1. Feasibility of EBS for UHTCs
Metal Boride UHTCs are unusual materials that possess ceramic-like properties such as high melting point, hardness, as well as metal-like properties such as high electrical and thermal conductivity. The UHTC material chosen for this research is ZrB2, which has an electrical and thermal conductivity of 1.08 × 10 7 S/m and 60 W/mK, respectively, and a melting temperature of 3245 ◦ C. With ZrSi2  as a sintering additive (melting point: 1625 ◦ C), it can be selectively melted, forming a liquid phase that binds the ZrB2  powder particles together, resulting in processing temperature around 1700 ◦ C that is easily attainable by the EBM machines.  Additionally,  ZrSi2  softens  around  800 ◦ C,  hence  a  liquid phase sintering process would most likely occur at temperatures less than 1700 ◦ C by an intergranular viscous ZrSi2 phase [11].
In the current study, a representative ZrB2 UHTC was mixed with 30 vol% of ZrSi2, which acts as a densification aid. It was determined by S. Guo  et  al.  [10]  that  in  the  traditional  UHTC  sintering  process,  the addition of ZrSi2  in the 30% volume fraction is critical in maintaining good  electrical  (11.9 × 10 6 S/m)  and  thermal  conductivities  (86.81 W/mK)  without  compromising  the  mechanical  behavior.  The  high electrical conductivity of the UHTC materials makes them compatible with the EBM process. The earlier work in our group [2] was the first of its  kind  to  demonstrate  the  feasibility  of  EBM  to  process  the  UHTC mixture  and  pioneer  EBS.  Although  only  1D  melt  tracks  have  been performed, interesting consolidation mechanisms have been reported. The current study will contribute more to the findings through experimentation and subsequent characterization of single-layer builds of the ZrB2 -30 vol% ZrSi2 powder feedstock.

Effect of Transition Metal Silicides
The extremely high melting point and the low self-diffusion coefficient make these ceramics very difficult to sinter to full density: temperatures above 2000°C and the application of pressure are necessary conditions. However these processing parameters lead to coarse microstructures, with mean grain size of the order of 20 μm and trapped porosity, all features which prevent the achievement of the full potential of the thermo-mechanical properties of UHTCs.
Several  activities  have  been  performed  in  order  to  decrease  the  severity  of  the processing conditions of UHTCs introducing sintering additives, such as metals, nitrides, carbides or silicides. In general the addition of such secondary phases does decrease the sintering temperature, but some additives have some drawbacks, especially during use at high temperature, owing to their softening and the following loss of integrity of the material.
In this chapter, composites based on borides and carbides of Zr, Hf and Ta were produced with addition of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 . These silicides were selected as sintering aids owing to their high melting point (>2100°C), their ductility above 1000°C and their capability to increase the oxidation resistance.
The microstructure of fully dense hot pressed UHTCs containing 15 vol% of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 ,  was  characterized by x-ray diffraction, scanning, and transmission electron microscopy.
Based on microstructural features detected by TEM, thermodynamical calculations, and the available phase diagrams, a densification mechanism for these composites is proposed.
The mechanical properties, namely hardness, fracture toughness, Young's modulus and flexural strength at room and high temperature, were measured and compared to the properties of other ultra-high temperature ceramics produced with other sintering additives. Further, the microstructural findings were used to furnish possible explanations for the excellent high temperature performances of these composites.

A B S T R A C T
strength and good thermal conductivity, the most studied UHTCs are the ZrB2-based composites [17 -19]. In order to improve the thermo-mechanical properties, oxidation resistance and final density, borides are usually combined with silicon-containing additives in small amount,  such  as  10 -20  vol%  of  SiC  [20,21].  In  fact,  the  oxidation resistance of pure ZrB2  is poor already at 1000 ◦ C, because of the formation of a porous and non-protective ZrO2 film, as well as the evaporation  of  B2O3.  The  introduction of  SiC  promotes  the  formation  of  a surficial glassy silica phase, which protects the material from oxidation up to 1600 ◦ C [22]. Other ceramic additives, such as Y2O3  or MoSi2 improve the densification of ZrB2-SiC composites by forming a liquid phase with the oxide impurities present on the surface of ZrB2 and SiC grains [23,24]. The presence of Y2O3 stabilizes the ZrO2 formed during oxidation in the tetragonal structure, leading to the formation of a more compact film that improves the oxidation resistance [25]. The presence of MoSi2 improves the oxidation resistance thanks to the formation of a SiO2 layer. In more detail, oxidation produces a SiO2-rich glass layer, a subsurface oxide layer, and a MoB, ZrO2, and SiO2 mixture layer [26].
In this work, the possibility of producing graded composites with a fibre gradient ranging from 0 to 50 vol% via hot pressing sintering was investigated. Three UHTC matrices were mixed with carbon fibres:
- ZrB2 -10 vol% SiC - 5 vol% Y2O3 where SiC is the oxidation resistant phase and Y2O3 is the sintering aid [4,27].
- ZrB2 -15 vol% MoSi2 where MoSi2 is both sintering and oxidation resistant aid [28,29].

A B S T R A C T
In this work, ZrB2 composites were produced containing high volumes, 40 vol%, of short carbon fiber plus 10 vol% of oxidation retardant phases. The latter consisted of 5 vol% of SiC combined with 5 vol% of one of three different MeSi2 compounds, viz. MoSi2, HfSi2 or WSi2. The selection of these silicides was based on their compatibility with high-temperature environments, i.e. they offer melting points above 1600 °C, high stiffness and, owing to their beneficial effects on densification, high temperature strength and potential oxidation resistance improvement for borides [23,25-28]. Additionally, a comparative material without MeSi2 but containing the same total amount of SH additives, i.e. 10 vol% SiC, was also prepared as a reference baseline. The question posed by this work was whether the replacement of 5 vol% of SiC with 5 vol% of MeSi2 would significantly improve the ablation resistance. Thus the UHTCMCs were exposed to the flame of an oxyacetylene torch (OAT) at temperatures above 2000 °C for 60 s. The analysis of the as-sintered and oxidized materials is presented and discussed, with the focus being on the effect of the secondary phases in promoting the formation of an ablation resistant microstructure.
The novelty of this work lies in the production and successful testing of a new class of UHTCMCs that can survive extreme conditions of oxidation at up to 2000 °C.

A B S T R A C T
In this work, ZrB2 composites were produced containing high volumes, 40 vol%, of short carbon fiber plus 10 vol% of oxidation retardant phases. The latter consisted of 5 vol% of SiC combined with 5 vol% of one of three different MeSi2 compounds, viz. MoSi2, HfSi2 or WSi2. The selection of these silicides was based on their compatibility with high-temperature environments, i.e. they offer melting points above 1600 °C, high stiffness and, owing to their beneficial effects on densification, high temperature strength and potential oxidation resistance improvement for borides [23,25-28]. Additionally, a comparative material without MeSi2 but containing the same total amount of SH additives, i.e. 10 vol% SiC, was also prepared as a reference baseline. The question posed by this work was whether the replacement of 5 vol% of SiC with 5 vol% of MeSi2 would significantly improve the ablation resistance. Thus the UHTCMCs were exposed to the flame of an oxyacetylene torch (OAT) at temperatures above 2000 °C for 60 s. The analysis of the as-sintered and oxidized materials is presented and discussed, with the focus being on the effect of the secondary phases in promoting the formation of an ablation resistant microstructure.
The novelty of this work lies in the production and successful testing of a new class of UHTCMCs that can survive extreme conditions of oxidation at up to 2000 °C.

Production of UHTC Complex Shapes and Architectures
(B) are stacked in alternate sequence (ZrBZrBZrB). The strong covalent bonding between B-B and Zr-B atoms results in high melting temperature, hardness and strength. The high electrical and thermal conductivity are due to the close packed Zr layers that show a metallic-like bonding.
Densification of UHTCs is extremely difficult due to its high melting point, strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion rates (Sonber & Suri, 2011). In the case of borides, oxide layers of B 2 O3 and Zr (Hf-) O 2 covering the surface of boride  particles  are  subjected  to  evaporation-condensation  mechanisms  at  low temperature resulting in mass transfer without densification. At higher temperature grain coarsening becomes predominant over densification and finally causes poor mechanical properties. Temperature above 1800°C and high external pressure are needed to achieve densification in monolithic borides thanks to the activation of grain boundary and volume diffusion mechanisms. Alternatively, densification of borides can be performed at lower temperatures and pressure through either addition of sintering agents or decrease of powder mean grain size and/or increase of the defect concentration through mechanical activation (high energy milling).
Sintering additives may be ceramics or metals. They can either form a liquid phase at the sintering temperature (liquid phase sintering) or may react with and remove oxidized surface film from boride powder particles inducing a solid state sintering densification.

1. Introduction
All the so-called ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs; i.e., borides, carbides, and nitrides of the early-transition metals [1]) have poor intrinsic sinterability due to the serious kinetic restrictions imposed by their strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coef /uniFB01 cients. This is further aggravated extrinsically because the starting powders normally comprise large particles of various microns in size and with surface oxide impurities. Not surprisingly, dedicated sintering studies constitute a large part of the research effort carried out on UHTCs, and particularly on ZrB2-based UHTCs [2,3]. They have enabled the identi /uniFB01 cation of appropriate sintering additives and comminution practices. Some of these suitable additives are carbon [4,5], B4C [6], WC [7,8], and B4C þ C [9,10], which favor the conventional solid-state sintering of

UHTCs by the carbothermal reduction of the surface metal oxides. This has been proven both for pressureless sintering (PS) and hot-pressing (HP), both of which apply prolonged sintering cycles (i.e., various hours). Apart from facilitating the conventional sintering, cleaning oxides off of the grain boundaries also results in improved high-temperature strength [5,8]. Furthermore, solid-state sintering additives are normally used synergistically with the wet-attrition milling of the starting powders because solid-state sinterability scales inversely with a power of the particle size (i.e., Herring's scaling law [11]).

Comprehensive study on the effect of SiC and carbon additives on the pressureless sintering and microstructural and mechanical characteristics of new ultra-high temperature ZrB2 ceramics
Based on preliminary studies by Mallik et al. [30], it has been reported that the ZrB2 -SiC composites with higher reinforcement content depict greater densi /uniFB01 cation indicating that besides the sintering additives, the SiC particles could have a role as well which needs to be fully understood. The effect of SiC volume fraction on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior needs to be examined synergistically with emphasis on understanding of the operative mechanisms of additives. Moreover, there is need to arrive at optimum temperature and duration of sintering, so as to obtain desired combination of relative density, microstructure and mechanical properties. Therefore, the present report is focused on studies comprising pressureless sintering of the ZrB2 -(2 -10 vol%) SiC composites at 2000 1 C for 2 h in order to examine the effects of carbon additive, as well as SiC content on their densi /uniFB01 cation, matrix grain size, and mechanical properties.

5. Densification, microstructure and mechanical properties
As discussed in the previous section, various sintering techniques (both conventional and advanced) have been used to densify transition metal di-boride based materials, with the monolithic di-borides usually necessitating very high sintering temperatures. The reduction of starting powders particle size and the use of sinter-additives are possibly the major approaches that are used to address this issue (see Figs. 7 and 9). Both metallic and non-metallic sinter-additives have been used to obtain fully dense di-boride ceramics at low sintering temperatures. While selecting the sinter-additive, the type and amount must be judiciously chosen as they influence densification, microstructure, phase assemblage and properties of the di-borides. Metallic additives (generally < 2 wt% added to borides) such as Ni, Cu, Co, Cr and Fe etc. are advantageous towards obtaining near-theoretical sinter-density, reducing the sintering temperature considerably due to liquid phase sintering and very effective in improving room temperature mechanical properties of borides [5,9,10,50,204]. However, discussion on the use of metallic sinter-additives is not included here since the addition of metallic additives degrades the high temperature properties of the di-boride ceramics, eventually rendering them unsuitable for the ultra-high temperature applications. For example, the strength of 4 wt% Ni added ZrB2 increased from 370 to 630 MPa with increasing temperature (up to 800 °C) and further increasing the temperature resulted drastic reduction in strength (240 MPa at 1000 °C and 20 MPa at 1200 °C) [10]. Hence, the effects of non-metallic sinter-additives on the densification and microstructure of the important di-boride ceramics have been discussed in the following sub-sections.

UHTC ceramics derived from B 4 C and MAX phases by reactive sintering
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a group of ceramic materials that is suitable for operation at very high temperatures, owing to their relatively high melting points, reaching values above 3000 °C 1 .  Most often, UHTCs are characterized mainly by their high thermal stability, high thermal conductivity, high hardness, and low density 2-4 .  Therefore, these materials have recently been considered as potential for used in extreme environments as resources for aircraft, spacecraft, nuclear reactors and others prospective applications 5 . Among these, boron-based systems have been studied because of their mechanical resistance, corrosion resistance and melting temperatures above 2500 °C. There are two types of boron-based UHTC carbides (e.g.B 4 C) and borides (for example, TiB 2 ) 6-8 .
The use of the described materials is limited by two factors: the low relative density of the final product and the high sintering temperature. Relative density is very important when any properties of covalent materials are considered. Both thermal and mechanical resistance are highly influenced by the amount of porosity introduced by the specific manufacture technology, which is an important research aspect in many reports 9-12 . High sintering temperatures are important in terms of industrial application of UHTCs materials due to possible reduction of energy consumption, which is very desired in the incoming years 13 . Several additives were considered in order to reduce the sintering temperatures and simultaneously improve density of borides and boron-based carbides. Those included: Al 2 O 3 , MoSi 2 , C, Y 2 O 3 , bringing various results and materials with mixed phases that influence their final properties 14-17 .

Processing Methods for Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Despite the  low-intrinsic  sinterability,  which  is  attributed  to  highly  covalent bonds and low volume and grain boundary diffusion rates, significant advances in pressureless sintering of UHTCs have been made. Various additives have been incorporated to improve the densification of ZrB 2 and HfB 2 . Metals, carbon, carbides and silicides have been used to enhance the densification. Addition of carbon and carbides (B 4 C, SiC, TiC, WC) assists in densification of borides to a density of more than 95% ρ th at temperature around 2000-2100 o C. These additives react with surface
Figure 1. Induction furnace
oxide layer and thus enhance the surface energy thereby improving densification. Metals (Fe, Ni) and silicides (MoSi 2 , ZrSi 2 ) improve the densification by forming liquid  phases  during  sintering.  The  additives  should  not  have  very  high  vapour pressure otherwise it leads to porosity in the final product.
Pressureless sintering may be useful for fabrication of shapes where high density is not required. Some thermal protection systems may not require very highmechanical strengths and density. The material porosity may even provide some benefit by lowering the stiffness and the thermal conductivity of the part.
