3.3.2. ZS20La
Reaction was complete or SiO2 was completely eliminated at a La2O3/SiO2 wt.% ratio of 3.2. 45 Two different polymorphs of La2Si2O7 were observed with a wt.% ratio of 6.4, which corresponds to a 4 wt.% La2O3/MoSi2 composites ( ∼ 5 vol.%). Therefore, in the present study La2O3 is completely dissolved in the La2O3-doped UHTCs BS melt for <5 vol.% La2O3content andprotective Me2La2O7 is not formed unless La2O3 concentration is at least 10 vol.%. 28 However, precipitates of La2O3-SiO2 phase were found on top of the oxide layer and identified with EDS in samples oxidized at 1500 ◦ C as observed in Fig. 7a indicated by the arrow, which confirms higher B2O3 volatilization at 1500 ◦ C than at 1400 ◦ C, facilitating the precipitation of La2O3-SiO2 particles. Furthermore, a completely new phenomena was observed on samples oxidized at 1600 ◦ C.
Fig. 6. SEM micrographs of oxide scale of ZS20 at 1600 ◦ C for a holding time of: (a) 1 h and (b) 2 h.
Fig. 7. SEM micrographs of oxide scale of ZS20La at 1500 ◦ C for a holding time of: (a) 8 h and (b) 16 h. Arrow indicates precipitation of La2O3-SiO2 particles.

3. Results
All samples attained high density values, higher than 99% of theoretical density (TD) except ZrB2 which was 95.8%. Theoretical density was calculated using the rule of mixtures. Table 2 shows SPS conditions and density values measured after sintering. Phase analysis by XRD confirmed all starting phases remained in UHTCs after SPS.
Fig. 1 shows secondary electron (SE) images of ground, polished and etched UHTCs surfaces after SPS. ZrB2 are the larger grey grains (2-6 𝛍 m), finer dark grains are SiC (1-2 𝛍 m) and light grey grains are La2O3 ( ∼ 1 𝛍 m) difficult to distinguish from ZrB2 grains, which are, respectively, labelled with 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 1c as they were confirmed with EDS. Homogeneous dispersion of SiC in ZS20 and SiC/La2O3 in ZS20La is found in the ZrB2 matrix. SiC and La2O3 are usually located together forming small agglomerates as an interconnected network in the ZrB2 and HfB2 matrix. However, less than 5% of SiC and La2O3 particles were detected separately. On the other hand, SiC agglomerates as large as 20-30 𝛍 m were found in HfB2-based composites as seen in Fig. 1b indicated by an arrow.

3. Results and discussion
The microstructures of the Cf-ZS20 and Cf-ZS20-1La composites were more or less similar to those of the Cf-ZrB2 composites. Two additional features observed on the surface of the Cf-ZS20 composite are shown in Fig. 14. The porosity in what had been molten droplets at higher temperature, Fig. 14a, is believed to be due to the escape of B2O3, SiO and CO/CO2 gases generated as a result of the oxidation of ZrB2 and SiC respectively. The formation of glassy microstructures on the surface, Fig. 14b, indicated the formation of borosilicate glasses away from the flame tip, where the sample experienced <2000 ◦ C.
The microstructure of the Cf-HfB2 composite subjected to 60 s oxyacetylene torch testing is shown in Fig. 15. This composition offered the best high temperature oxidation protection, even though the powder impregnation was not as good as that achieved for the ZrB2-based composites, as discussed earlier. Fig. 15a reveals the presence of what had been molten HfO2 and Fig. 15b reveals fibre degradation that occurred directly below the flame tip. Fig. 15c shows a high magnification image on one of the frozen droplets and Fig. 15d shows a porous microstructure ∼ 1-2 mm away from the flame tip. It may be possible to reduce the extent of fibre damage by improving the HfB2 impregnation and experiments are underway to achieve this. UHTC powder with finer particle sizes is required to improve further the impregnation.
Fig. 15. Microstructure after oxyacetylene torch testing of a Cf-ZS20 composite. (a) Porosity observed on the frozen droplets which had been molten at the test temperature and (b) glassy structure formed on the composite ∼ 10 mm from the flame tip.

3.3.2. ZS20La
The presence of La2O3 within the BS melt increases its viscosity and reduces the O2 and CO2 diffusion coefficients, so promoting reaction (8) to take place at 1600 ◦ C. In addition, ZrO2 x C x could react and be oxidized by atmospheric O2 forming an active-protective barrier for inwards oxygen diffusion. The adsorption of species is also noticeable and confirmed by the difference between mass gain kinetics and oxide layer thickness kinetics. Moreover, a large mass gain change produces a relatively small increase of oxide scale thickness as a consequence of oxidation kinetics exponent between mass gain and oxide layer (2 and 8, respectively), as seen in Fig. 2. An oxidation kinetic exponent as high as 8 could be obtained if Fick's law for atomic diffusion is constrained by the presence of a particle sink. 46 Furthermore, the more particles formed within the oxide scale, the more limited the oxygen diffusion. The continuous oxidation of ZrO x C y and SiO x C y particles and continuous recombination or possible adsorption of oxygen-containing species explain how an appreciable change in mass gain produce a small change in oxide layer thickness, addressing the difference in measured values for oxidation kinetics exponents (Table 1). However, the spatial distribution of ZrO2 and ZrO x C y is not clear as it is likely there is more than one mechanism forming ZrO x C y ; this is the subject of further investigation. Therefore, ZrO x C y coatings are presented as new candidates for UHTCs protection against oxidation. Fig. 9 shows an overview of ZS20La oxide layer formed at 1600 ◦ C after 4 h oxidation.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited what high-temperature strength at 1800°C, and how did this compare to the undoped version?
answer: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited a high-temperature strength of 390 ± 21 MPa at 1800°C. This compared to the undoped version, which had a high-temperature strength of 34 ± 6 MPa at 1800°C [6][7].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. 1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics.  Thus,  there  is  a

4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

From the stress-strain  curves,  the  load  of  ZC1600-2h  increased  almost  linearly until reaching a maximum value, and then sharply decreased after the final  brittle  fracture. In contrast, the ZS15 specimen yielded at only approximately 30 MPa, and then steadily deformed with a large strain at a low stress of 30 -34 MPa. The low yield strength was attributed to the presence of Zr -Si phases with a low-melting point. It has been reported that the low melting point phases would significantly reduce the  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs  [38,39].  After flexural test, the ZS15 specimen was obviously bent and not completely broken into two pieces, as displayed in Fig. 6(c). The high-temperature flexural strength of ZC1600-2h was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was an order of  magnitude higher than ZS15 (34 ± 6 MPa), as shown in Table 2. Compared  to  its  room-temperature  strength  of  494 ± 2  MPa,  the high-temperature strength retention ratio (the ratio of 1800 ◦ C-strength to RT-strength) of ZC1600-2h was as high as ~79%, which was much higher than that of ZS15 (~6%).

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
The flexural strength of hot pressed ZrB2 tested between room temperature and 1600 ◦ C showed a value of 360 MPa at room temperature decreasing to a minimum of 170 MPa. The same ceramic when tested in Ar  between  1500 ◦ C  and  2300 ◦ C  raised  its  strength  to  300  MPa  at 2200 ◦ C, which then lowered down to 220 MPa at 2300 ◦ C [151]. A historic study by Rhodes [143] demonstrated the increase in bending strength  of  ZrB2  from  325  MPa  at  room  temperature  to  420  MPa  at 800 ◦ C. Further, increase in temperature reduced the strength to 145 MPa at 1400 ◦ C.  Bend strength again raised to 200 MPa at 1900 ◦ C followed by a drop at 2200 ◦ C to 50 MPa. The initial rise in bend strength was ascribed to the thermal residual stresses relieving at higher temperatures. The second rise in bend strength was attributed to the plastic flow from creep, again relieving the stresses [143]. ZrB2  ceramic composite (with 5 vol % WC + 3 vol % SiC) developed by Silvestroni et al. [152] retained a flexural strength of 836 MPa at 1800 ◦ C. The elevated temperature bend strength of some ZrB2  and HfB2  based ceramics is illustrated  in  Fig.  18.  Particulate  reinforced  di-borides  especially  SiC

In order to simultaneously achieve low-temperature preparation and good high-temperature resistance, it is needed to remove the residual ZrSi2 from the final ZrB2-based ceramic. In the previous studies [32,33], we found that introducing carbonaceous material into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  can reduce the amount of residual ZrSi2  in the final materials, due to the reaction between carbonaceous material and sintering additive of ZrSi2. However, the effects of sintering additive transformation on the densification  process,  phase  composition,  microstructure  and  mechanical properties are still unclear.
In the current study, active carbon was introduced into ZrB2 -ZrSi2 to prepare high-temperature-resistant ZrB2-based ceramics at a relatively low temperature via in situ reactive sintering. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  were  hot-pressed  at  different  sintering  temperatures.  Phase composition, microstructure, densification behavior and hightemperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs were systematically investigated, in order to clarify the effects of active carbon on the densification and high-temperature properties of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.

4. Discussion
ceramics studied to date (217 MPa at 1800 ◦ C) for matrix grain size of 8 𝛍 m. However, plastic deformation occurred at 1800 ◦ C for matrix grain sizes lower than 5 𝛍 m. Elastic behaviour with relatively high strength was found with increasing ZrB2 grain size introducing a microstructural tailoring factor that controls the high temperature mechanical response. A systematic study of grain size effect and SiC content on high temperature strength of ZrB2 based ceramics is required to understand the mechanical responses and failure mechanisms of these systems. In addition, this is an effect that has been characterised for room temperature strength. The effect of sintering method on mechanical properties has been analysed. Pressureless sintered ZrB2-20 MoSi2 had higher strength at 1500 ◦ C than hot-pressed ZrB2-20 MoSi2 while the room temperature strengths remained similar. In addition, hot-pressed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 and SPSed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 have similar room and high temperature strength. This effect is explained in terms of the secondary phases formed during hot-pressing and SPS of these materials, such as SiO2 and ZrO2 which soften the grain boundaries at high temperatures, especially the residual silica pockets. The addition of carbon to the system avoided the residual silica pocket formation during hot-pressing increasing substantially high temperature strength of ZrB2-15 MoSi2. In the present study, the general trend is that HfB2-based UHTCs had higher flexural strength at high temperature than ZrB2-based UHTCs (Tables 6 and 7). The strength at 1400 ◦ C of ZS20 and ZS20La and HS20 and HS20La is approximately twice that of ZrB2 and HfB2, 37 respectively. The reduction of the strength at 1400 ◦ C in the La2O3-doped UHTCs could be attributed to La2O3 inducing grain boundary softening.

3.3. Comparison amongst the composites
Finally, even the correlation between porosity and strength of the C 3 HARME composites agree with the composite literature [75]. The lack of  correlation  ( r ≅ 0)  in  case  of  flexural  strength  at  1800 ◦ C can be explained by the significantly higher strength of CFC material due to its higher thermomechanical stability (i.e . less prone to softening mechanisms) and release of RTSs. The matrix seems to suggest some correlation  ( r ≅ | 0 . 6 | )  between  the  stiffness  of  the  fibres  and  the  flexural strength of the composites. The inverse correlation at RT suggests that the fibre ' s stiffness decreased the apparent interlaminar shear strength of  the  composites  leading  to  a  lower  flexural  strength.  In  fact,  this decrease was not observed with the tensile tests ( r < | 0 . 2 | ) where the interlaminar  shear  stresses  are  negligible.  This  result  agrees  with  a previous work, Ref. [33], where we observed a decrease of apparent interlaminar shear strength with the increase of the composite ' s stiffness as consequence of the different span-to-thickness ratio. At 1800 ◦ C this effect is hidden and exceeded by the strength gain due to the higher thermomechanical  stability  (i.e . the  composites  with  higher  stiffness were  less  prone  to  softening  mechanisms).  Finally,  as  expected  the strength of the C 3 HARME composites shows a good correlation with the strength and the elongation of the fibres.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), how did the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolve from 1350°C to 1600°C during hot-press sintering, and what was the final state of the Zr-Si-C phases?
answer: In Liu et al. (2023), the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolved as follows during hot-press sintering: at 1350°C, the polished surface was quite rough with a lot of pores, and the loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr-Si-C cluster [1]. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450°C, the pores became less, and the Zr-Si-C cluster precipitated spheres with a diameter of ~85 nm [1]. After hot-sintering at 1600°C for 30 min, the Zr-Si-C cluster transformed into a core-shell phase, with the core being carbide enriched Zr and the shell being carbide enriched Si [1]. The final state of the Zr-Si-C phases was nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles [2]. The information about the final state of the Zr-Si-C phases is not explicitly detailed in the provided sources.

chunks.text:
3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
Fig. 3 shows the typical morphologies on the polished surface of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at different temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the polished surface of ZC1350 was quite rough with a lot of pores. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450 ◦ C, the pores became less (Fig. 3(c)). In comparison, the polished surfaces of ZC1600 and ZC1600-2h were flat with almost no pores (Fig. 3(e, g)). Fig.  3(b,  d,  f,  h)  shows  the  high  magnification  images  to  reveal  the morphological  evolution.  After  hot-sintering  at  1350 ◦ C,  the  loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr -Si -C cluster, as marked by the dotted ellipse in Fig. 3(b). Based on the reaction between the siliceous component and C (Reaction 4 -6), the Zr -Si -C cluster may contain ZrSi2,  ZrSi,  Si,  ZrC,  and  SiC.  It  is  difficult  to  distinguish  the various phases. With the sintering temperature increasing to 1450 ◦ C, the  Zr -Si -C cluster  precipitated  spheres  with  a  diameter  of  ~85  nm (Fig. 3(d)). After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), the Zr -Si -C cluster tfransformed into a core-shell phase (as marked by the dotted lines). Based on the micro-area analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3(f)), the core was carbide enriched Zr, and the shell was carbide enriched Si. The reactivity of Zr species with carbonaceous materials is higher than that of Si species, as can be confirmed in the erosion of carbon  fiber  by  molten  ZrSi2 [35,36],  tending  to  form  a  similar core-shell  structure  [37]. 4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.

1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

Notably, the enthalpy change calculated from the peak area during 1200 -1400 ◦ C for mixed powders was 1127 J/g, which was much larger than 403 J/g of the pure ZrSi2 powders. Fig.  2  shows X-ray diffraction patterns of the C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  and  as-sintered  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.  As  shown  in Fig. 2, for the powders, the diffraction peaks of ZrSi2  and ZrB2  phases were clearly detected, while C was difficult to identify due to its amorphous state. After sintering at 1350 -1600 ◦ C, besides the primary ZrB2 phase,  the  diffraction  peaks  of  ZrC  and  SiC  appeared  with  the disappearance of ZrSi2  peaks, indicating the occurrence of the in-situ reactions mentioned above. With the sintering temperature increased, the intensity of ZrC and SiC diffraction peaks gradually increased, which implied the in-situ reactions were gradually deepening during 1350 -1600 ◦ C.

3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
In order to understand phase evolution during the in-situ reactive sintering, heat and mass changes of the mixed powders during heating process,  phase  composition  and  microstructure  of  the  as-sintered  Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics were successively investigated. Fig. 1 shows the curves of heat change vs. temperature and mass change vs. temperature for the mixed C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders. As can be seen in Fig. 1, three distinguished exothermic peaks were observed. The first exothermic peak was found at around 921 ◦ C, which may be caused by the exothermic coverage of the reaction between ZrSi2 and carbon. The second  and  third  exothermic  peaks  were  located  closely  at  around 1311 ◦ C and 1347 ◦ C, which should be related to two different reactions at 1300 -1400 ◦ C. It is well known that ZrSi2 becomes unstable with the temperature up to near its melting temperature. The formation enthalpy of ZrSi2 and ZrSi were GLYPH<0> 60 kJ/mol and GLYPH<0> 90 kJ/mol, respectively [34]. In order to simultaneously achieve low-temperature preparation and good high-temperature resistance, it is needed to remove the residual ZrSi2 from the final ZrB2-based ceramic. In the previous studies [32,33], we found that introducing carbonaceous material into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  can reduce the amount of residual ZrSi2  in the final materials, due to the reaction between carbonaceous material and sintering additive of ZrSi2. However, the effects of sintering additive transformation on the densification  process,  phase  composition,  microstructure  and  mechanical properties are still unclear.
In the current study, active carbon was introduced into ZrB2 -ZrSi2 to prepare high-temperature-resistant ZrB2-based ceramics at a relatively low temperature via in situ reactive sintering. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  were  hot-pressed  at  different  sintering  temperatures.  Phase composition, microstructure, densification behavior and hightemperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs were systematically investigated, in order to clarify the effects of active carbon on the densification and high-temperature properties of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.

2.2. Atmospheric Re-entry
Starting at a 120 km altitude and an initial ~ Mach 25 velocity, NASA space shuttles were required to end missions by landing on a runway 91 m wide [33], - a challenge for blunt-shaped bodies. The accuracy of a body during re-entry is related to leading edge geometry. Sharp geometries have higher ballistic coefficients (BC) and lower drag forces/coefficients than blunt geometries. However, as BC increases, so too do maximum heating rates. If a material can withstand ultra-high temperatures, the BC of the vehicle can be increased via sharper, more streamlined geometries that allow several advantages, like significantly altering the altitude of maximum deceleration. The lower the altitude of deceleration, the shorter the amount of time spent in the atmosphere, or the more rapidly the vehicle arrives at the surface of the Earth. The benefits of a high BC compared to a low BC are listed in Table 2.
Opeka et al. indicated the 500 ◦ -2000 ◦ C range as being the necessary materials window for hypersonics [34]. Technically, hypersonic velocity is defined as any speed exceeding the speed of sound, but is typically more  specifically  defined  as  beginning  at  Mach  5,  or  in  the  range 1702-3403 m/s. Hypersonic velocities are less demanding the further from the Earth ' s atmosphere due to decreasing air density. The Space Shuttle re-entering the Earth ' s  atmosphere at nearly Mach 25 in low earth  orbit  is  a  substantially  different  thermal,  aerodynamic,  and oxidation scenario than Mach 25 a few kilometers above the surface.
As discussed earlier, the maximum usage temperature for any SiC based thermal protection system (TPS) is ~1727 ◦ C and for comparative purposes, SiC is viewed as protective up to1727 ◦ C. The  existing  thermogravimetric  and  wide  temperature  range  oxidation tests have verified the nonlinear relationship between the selfhealing ability of coating oxide films under temperature gradients and temperature. However, the linear temperature change mode is difficult to  reproduce  the  transient  competition  mechanism  of  thermal  shock and oxidation coupling during space reentry, which limits the accuracy of  predicting  the  oxygen  diffusion  path  of  coatings  under  extreme thermal gradients.

THERMAL SHOCK RESISTANCE
After the recent tragic explosion of Space Shuttle Columbia, the findings of the Columbia Accident Investigation Board and the recent Space Exploration Initiative suggest that safety be made the top priority in the design of new reentry vehicles (Wang et al., 2007). During reentry from Low Earth Orbit (LEO), such as from the Space Station or parking orbits, relatively high speeds (on the order of 8 km/s) are reached (Monteverde et al., 2007). External thermal protection systems of reusable space vehicles, in the form of rigid surfaces in areas of ultra-high working temperatures (beyond 1800 °C), must be able to withstand high temperatures, high heat flux and mechanical stresses associated with vibrations at launch and re-entry in Earth's atmosphere (Tang et al., 2007). Most ceramic matrix composites, including ZrB 2 matrix ceramics, have inherently low fracture toughness and are thus susceptible to catastrophic fractures caused by thermal stresses under the above-mentioned conditions. Consequently, the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic must be estimated.
In  the  present  work,  the  thermal  shock  resistance  of  the  ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was investigated by the water quenching method, and the microstructure of the specimen before and after water quenching was observed. Furthermore, the temperature and thermal stress distributions in the specimen during instantaneous water quenching were simulated and analyzed in detail by finite element analysis.

1. Introduction
With the increased severity of hypersonic re-entry vehicles ' operating  conditions,  the  propulsion  system  requires  materials  that  can withstand  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C  [1].  Ceramic  matrix  composites (CMCs) such as C/C and C/SiC fibers are the existing materials of choice due  to  their  light-weight,  excellent  mechanical,  and  thermal  shock resistance [2]. However, carbon is susceptible to oxidation above 400 ◦ C and has poor stability in an erosive environment [3,4]. The addition of SiC only improves its stability until 1100 ◦ C due to the volatile nature of SiO2  at higher temperatures [5]. Due to the thermal limitations of C/C and  C/SiC,  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  considered good additives for conventional C/C composites. UHTCs are carbides, nitrides, and borides of early transition metal elements (group IV and V) with  a  combination  of  metal  and  ceramic-like  properties  that  offers excellent  stability  at  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C.  UHTC  is  a  potential candidate for a thermal protection system (TPS) for reusable re-entry vehicles. TPS  is necessary for spacecraft that enter the earth ' s

5. Conclusions
ZrB2-SiC based ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 were prepared by hot-pressing. By means of the water-quenching, thermal shock behavior of the ceramics was investigated with temperature difference increased from 200 to 800 ◦ C. Results of residual strength and critical temperature difference revealed that ZS10Z exhibited the optimal thermal shock resistance. Simulated analysis with FEM found that ZS10Z provided lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factors during thermal shock, which was in a good agreement with the experimental data. Improved toughness was beneficial for the thermal shock resistance. In addition, phase transformation from t-ZrO2 to m-ZrO2 during thermal shock led to the tougher surface, which was also favorable for the thermal shock resistance. With further addition of ZrO2 to ZrB2-SiC, phase transformation during thermal shock will crack the surface. In conclusion, ZrB2 -10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 wasdiscovered to provide the best thermal shock resistance, which should be instructive for the design of ZrB2 -based ultra-high temperature ceramics.

a b s t r a c t
Thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-10vol%SiC ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 was investigated by means of water-quenching as well as the finite element analysis. Optimal residual strength and critical temperature difference were obtained from ZrB2-10 vol%SiC ceramic added by 10vol%ZrO2. Simulated results revealed that ZrB2-10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 provided the lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factor during thermal shock, which confirmed the experimental data. Such improvement in thermal shock resistance compared with ZrB2-10vol%SiC was primarily ascribed to the improved toughness by the addition of ZrO2. Besides, appropriate phase transformation of ZrO2 during quenching was further beneficial to resist the thermal shock.

The present paper focused on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC based UHTCs with addition of zirconia. By means of traditional water-quenching as well as the finite element method (FEM), influence of ZrO2 on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC ceramics was analyzed.

1. Introduction
After  a  decade  of  research  on  ultra  high  temperature  ceramics (UHTCs), ZrB2 based materials have emerged as the potential candidate for  extreme  environments  associated  with  the  hypersonic  futuristic technology [1 -3]. ZrB2 based materials have high melting point ( > 3000 0 C [4]), high thermal conductivity (85 -90 W/m/K at room temperature [5]) and strength retention at high temperature [6,7]. These properties of  ZrB2 based  materials  conform  to  the  properties  of  the  materials required for the intended applications [1]. SiC has been often used with ZrB2 to make ZrB2 -SiC composites [4 -8]. However, the monolithic ZrB2 and ZrB2 -SiC composites have some limitations for practical application due to low fracture toughness and poor thermal shock resistance. The fracture toughness of the monolithic ZrB2 values ranges from 2 to 4 MPa m 1/2 , and increases marginally to 3.5 -5.5 MPa m 1/2 with SiC addition, i. e. for the ZrB2 -SiC composites [8]. Thermal shock severity ( Δ Tc) was reported to be 275 and 400 ◦ C for the monolithic ZrB2  and ZrB2 - 20% SiC composite, respectively [9]. This restricts the applicability of ZrB2 based  materials  to  large  structures.  Various  test  articles  of  ZrB2 -SiC composites after flight and arc jet tests were found damaged severely [10].  To  overcome  these  limitations,  UHTCs  require  conversion  to  a more damage tolerant material. One way to produce damage tolerant

4. Conclusions
In conclusion, ZrB2-20%SiC and ZrB2-20%SiC-10%graphite ultrahigh temperature ceramic were fabricated. A novel method was proposed to characterize thermal shock resistance for ZS. Under the experimental condition in this work, critical temperature at the center of specimen is w 2300 /C14 C for ZS and 2400 /C14 C for ZS10C. By several repeating thermal shock experiments, it implies that ZS10C provides a better repeating thermal shock resistance. It is calculated for thermal stress caused by temperature gradient and thermal expansion mismatch of phases, which is consistent with experiment for ZS.
The method proposed in this paper can be used to characterize UHTC failure with local place heated up quickly, and it may provide help for fabricating UHTC with higher application temperatures.

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Not only the mechanical properties and oxidation resistance, but responses of materials to the conditions of high enthalpy plasma flows that are typically encountered during atmospheric re-entry need to be evaluated. One of the possible ways to investigate such effects, in terms of materials' response to large heat fluxes as well as oxidation resistance under extreme/complex conditions, is Arc-jet testing. A schematic representation of a typical arc-jet testing set-up is presented in Figure 14a. In a recent work, Savino et al. (Savino, 2008) had performed arc-jet testing using a 80 kW plasma torch, operating under in inert gas conditions at mass flow rates up to 5 g/s, on hemispheric shaped HfB 2 - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 and HfC - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 specimens placed at a distance of 6 cm form the exit torch (see Figure 14bd).  The  temperatures of investigation were between 1950 o C and 2400 o C, which revealed that both the  materials  could  be  stable  up  to  temperatures  exceeding  2000 o C. Prior to this work, arc-jet testing were done only on ZrB 2 -  SiC and HfB 2 -  SiC composites (Gasch, 2004). Due to the issues associated with experimental determination of such high temperature properties, a lot of work has focused on theoretical simulation of the behavior via computational fluid dynamics (Borrelli, 2010), FEM based analysis (Borrelli, 2009; Stine, 1968) or various other computational tools. Such models need to simulate the demands for the application under consideration. However, despite the progress with experimental and computational facilities, still lacking  is  a  comprehensive  thermostructural  database  for  the  various  potential UHTCs.

2.1.1. Temperature profiles
The thermocouples inserted and glued in into the UHTC tip (TC 9, 10, and 14) recorded the highest temperature values due to elevated heating fluxes up to 9 MW/m 2 reached during the atmospheric re-entry phase (Fig. 3). The discontinuities observed on the thermal profiles at about 760 s are markings of the tip breaking which exposed the thermocouples to the external environment.
For what concerns the front shield and inner steel structure (Fig. 4), during the re-entry in earth atmosphere, the temperatures quickly raised reaching values up to about 500 ◦ C in the case of the thermocouples TC 1, 5, and 8 inserted inside the metallic structure in proximity of the ceramic nose tip.
The thermocouples on the back shield (TC 3, 4, and 15) experienced much lower temperatures because heat was transmitted mostly by conduction through the structure of the capsule (Fig. 5).

[174] reported an increase in the flexural strength of the sample with thermal shock cycles (514 MPa, 645 MPa, 494 MPa, and 589 MPa after 10, 20, 30, and 50 thermal shock cycles, respectively) when compared to that of the samples before any thermal shock (440 MPa). a b s t r a c t
operational temperatures of Ni-based superalloys are limited by the formation of low melting topologically close-packed phases, as well as a loss of creep resistance above 900 /C14 C. As these alloys approach their limits, the range of operational temperatures is expanded via active cooling and thermal barrier coatings. We recognize that the ZrB2-SiC composites have the potential for an in-situ natively grown thermal barrier, in the form of zirconia. Underneath this ' native ' thermal barrier, the presence of a silica rich scale will provide the oxidation resistance. Zirconia, however, has relatively poor thermal shock resistance. Failure of the zirconia through thermal shock, especially under cyclic conditions, is therefore identi /uniFB01 ed as a key factor that limits the reusability of the material system. Our concept is based around mitigating the shock by ensuring the presence of a subscale pliable enough to accommodate the thermal expansion and contraction of the overlying zirconia, yet tenacious enough to retain the zirconia grains. This is achieved by appropriately adjusting the viscosity of the underlying subscale, and builds on our previous work where we demonstrated addition of AlN can result in signi /uniFB01 cant modi /uniFB01 cations of the viscosity [8,9]. Past efforts have indicated that addition of AlN results in improved densi /uniFB01 cation of ZrB2-SiC composites under hot pressing conditions. Additionally, AlN is a high melting ceramic that has excellent thermal conductivity e which is certainly desirable in a thermal protection system.

gaps: the low end of Huang's thermal shock range

question: Comparing porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 with ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016: which material system reports the higher room-temperature strength value?
answer: The room-temperature strength value of porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 is reported to be 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa [6][9]. However, there is no information in the provided sources regarding the room-temperature strength value of ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016. Therefore, a comparison cannot be made based on the available information.

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics. In recent years, slurry injection technology has demonstrated potential for shortening process cycles in SI by directly introducing high-solid-content ceramic slurries into fiber preforms. 15-18 According to the study by Liu et al., 19 the process was optimized through the injection of ZrB2 -phenolic resin slurry, achieving a density of 2.10 ± 0.05 g/cm 3 and porosity of approximately 40%. However, subsequent densification processes are required after slurry injection into preforms. Venkatachalam et al. 20 fabricated C f -ZrB 2 -Cm composites using slurry injection combined with radio-frequency-assisted CVI, achieving 90% relative density for C f -ZrB 2 within 24 h. Under oxyacetylene ablation, the composites exhibited mass ablation rate of 1.7 mg/s and linear ablation rate of 1.6 µ m/safter 60 s (peak temperature ∼ 2600 K). However, residual 10% porosity in the 2.5D carbon fiber preform, caused by complex pore structures and CVI process limitations, significantly reduced ablation performance. Yang et al. 21 obtained C/ZrB2-SiC with 86% relative density and 215 MPa flexural strength using 30 vol.% ZrB 2 slurry injection combined with 12 PIP cycles. Hu et al. 22 achieved a 286 MPa flexural strength for Cf /ZrC-SiC through eight PIP cycles. Although PIP enables gradient distribution control of matrix phases, its requirement for multiple cycles (typically > 10) extends fabrication time to hundreds of hours. 23 RMI rapidly produces high-density ceramic matrix composites through molten metal reactions with preforms. It is particularly suitable for complex-shaped components, having demonstrated efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and high application potential. 24,25

1. Introduction
ZrB2-based composites have an extremely high melting point (>3000 /C176 C), high thermal and electrical conductivities, low density, and excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [1-4]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramic composites have been considered as potential candidates for application in critical thermal protection systems and other components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles [1-7]. Silicon carbide (SiC) is commonly used as an additive to improve the oxidation resistance and mechanical properties of ZrB2. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC particulate composites (ZS) are regarded as the baseline material in this research. However, unsatisfactory thermal shock resistance remains the obstacle to the development of ZrB2-SiC ceramic on an industrial scale. To avoid or mitigate catastrophic failure of ZrB2SiC ceramics during thermal shock, most current researches focus on composition design to improve the performance by selecting different additives [8-12]. Carbon is also sometimes used as an additive to enhance thermal shock resistance [12-14]. Recently, Levine et al. [13] studied the mechanical properties of ZrB214 vol% SiC-30 vol% C. They concluded that the addition of carbon could reduce strength but increases fracture toughness. Hong et al. [14] fabricated ZrB2-SiC-graphite composite, which exhibits increased thermal shock resistance compared with monolithic ZrB2-SiC. The toughness and hence the reliability and thermal

A B S T R A C T
Controlling crystallographic texture and pore architecture in porous ultra-high temperature ceramics is critical for tailoring their performance in extreme environments, yet achieving this dual control remains a formidable challenge.  Here,  we  demonstrate  a  novel  strategy  to  fabricate  architected  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  exhibiting strong crystallographic and mechanical property anisotropy by synergizing powder extrusion printing with in-situ reaction  synthesis.  The  core  of  our  approach  is  a  templated-growth  mechanism,  where  shear  forces  during extrusion  align  sacrificial  graphite  flakes  within  the  green  body.  These  aligned  flakes  subsequently  serve  as heterogeneous  nucleation  and  growth  sites  for  plate-like  YB2C2  grains  during  boro/carbothermal  reduction, effectively  translating  the  macro-scale  printed  path  into  a  micro-scale  crystallographic  texture.  This  process yields a remarkable hierarchical structure, featuring unidirectionally aligned pores and a strong [001] crystallographic texture parallel to the printing direction. The resulting ceramics exhibit a highly tunable porosity (50 -75 vol%) and exceptional compressive strength, reaching 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa at ~50 % porosity. Crucially, this microstructural control imparts a pronounced mechanical anisotropy, a key attribute for designing components with directionally dependent loads. This work establishes a new paradigm for manufacturing ultra-high temperature ceramics, enabling the concurrent design of geometry and crystallographic texture to achieve tailored thermomechanical properties.
when used as a reinforcement phase in Cu-based composites [5,6]. These properties position YB2C2 as an ideal material for structural components in harsh environments such as thermal protection systems, nuclear reactors, and hypersonic vehicle engines [7 -9]. However, realizing the full potential of porous YB2C2  ceramics hinges on the ability to precisely control  their  porosity,  crystallographic  texture,  and  macroscopic  geometry -a challenge that has yet to be fully addressed by conventional fabrication techniques.

1. Introduction
Porous ceramics with engineered anisotropy have attracted significant  attention  for  their  unique  combination  of  low  density,  tailored permeability, and direction-dependent mechanical and thermal properties, which are critical for advanced aerospace, energy, and environmental  applications  [1 -3].  Among  ultra-high-temperature  ceramics (UHTCs),  YB2C2  has  emerged  as  a  promising  candidate  owing  to  its exceptional thermal stability above 2500 ◦ C, excellent chemical inertness,  and  remarkable  resistance  to  thermal  shock  and  mechanical damage  [4].  For  instance,  YB2C2 retains  more  than  70 %  of  its room-temperature bending strength at 1300 ◦ C, and has demonstrated excellent chemical compatibility and enhanced mechanical performance

3.4. Composite mechanical behaviour
The composite (Cf-YB2C2) was machined into bars and tested under bending to evaluate the strength at room temperature. Compared to a reference material based on a ZrB2/SiC matrix reinforced with continuous carbon fibres (Cf-ZrB2/SiC) with similar carbon fibre content and porosity, the ycBC-Cf composite yielded higher values of strength, which were only matched and surpassed in the case of the Y2O3 doped ZrB2/SiC material, where the YB2C2 is formed during sintering within the ZrB2/ SiC matrix. This highlights the promising mechanical properties of the YB2C2 phase combined with the fibre reinforcement, and confirms the synergistic effect in the ZrB2/SiC/Y2O3 matrix which leads to a decrease of porosity and an increase of the mechanical properties.
From  the  load/displacement  curves  of  the  Cf-YB2C2 composite (Fig.  11  a),  the  behaviour  observed  is  typical  of  other  UHTCMCs previously reported, mostly constituted by an elastic zone, followed by the  progressive  breaking  of  the  layers.  The  fracture  surface  appears rough and complex (Fig. 11 b), and looking at the details of the fractured matrix (Fig. 11 c), fibres appear well anchored to the ceramic matrix and the fibre pull-out is very limited, implying a strong fibre/matrix interface; moreover, the individual lamellae can be seen at different angles in spite of the partial anisotropy imparted by the applied pressure during sintering.  From  Fig.  11  d,  the  individual  YB2C2  lamellae  themselves showed some sliding over each other and some degree of lamellae pullout, which likely contributed to the high strength reported.

3.3. Cycle dependence of thermal shock resistance
Fig. 7 shows the /uniFB02 exural strength of the C/C-ZrC composites experiencing di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles from 1700 °C to the room temperature. The /uniFB02 exural strength decreases gradually with the increasing number of thermal shock cycles. The residual strength of the composite thermally shocked for 20 cycles is about 130 MPa, 70.3% of the as-produced C/C-ZrC composite. Mass change of the C/C-ZrC composite with di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles was also measured and listed in Fig. 7. The mass loss keeps constant without obvious di /uniFB00 erence, which is the same as the composite thermally shocked at di /uniFB00 erent temperatures (< 1900 °C).
Fig. 7. Flexural strength and mass loss of the C/C-ZrC composites subjected to di /uniFB00 erent thermal shock cycles.
Fig. 8 shows the cross section microstructure of the C/C-ZrC composite thermally shocked from 1700 °C to the room temperature for di /uniFB00 erent thermal cycles. The formation of matrix cracks and interfacial debonding can be clearly observed as that shown in Fig. 5. With increasing numbers of thermal cycles, the interfacial debonding becomes more severe. The cross section microstructure of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent numbers is dense without formation of pores, which is consistent with the mass constant listed in Fig. 7. Here, the shocked temperature is merely 1700 °C, which is not high enough to enable the evaporation of the Zr2Si phase. Thus, no obvious weight change was measured.

4. Conclusion
Thermal shock resistance of the Zr-Si alloyed melt in /uniFB01 ltrated C/CZrC composite was tested using an induction heating equipment from ultra high temperatures (particularly > 1500 °C) to the room temperature. Both strength degradation and microstructure evolution were characterized in order to reveal the thermal shock induced damage to the composite. The following conclusions are obtained:
- (1) Flexural strength of the post thermal shocked C/C-ZrC composite slightly decreases initially without weight loss below 1300 °C, while it decreases with relatively high values and then sharply decreases over 1900 °C with obvious increase of weight loss.
- (2) Flexural strength of the post thermal shocked composites is dependence of thermal cycles. Flexural strength decreases gradually with the increasing number of thermal shocked cycles without obvious weight loss from 1700 °C to the room temperature.
- (3) Matrix cracking, interfacial debonding and matrix pores are the main reasons for the strength degradation of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent cycles and temperatures.

8.3.2  Strength and Fracture Toughness
8.3.2.2  Strength of ZrB 2 -SiC. The elevated-temperature flexure strength of several hot-pressed ZrB 2 -SiC ceramics with and without additives is shown in Figure 8.13. The ZrB 2 -20 vol% SiC studied by Rhodes [25] exhibited an increase in strength from 390 MPa at room temperature to 420 MPa at 800°C. The strength steadily decreased to 245 MPa at 1800°C and decreased more rapidly to 115 MPa at 2000°C. Modern ZrB 2 -SiC exhibited higher strengths at room and elevated temperature compared to Rhodes' material and similarly for all temperatures measured thus far. Zou et al . [90, 91] have reported the three-point bend strengths at elevated temperature for several compositions up to 1600°C in argon. Zou reported a strength of 550 MPa for ZrB 2 -20 vol% SiC at room temperature, increasing to 680 MPa at 1000°C, then steadily decreasing to 460 MPa at 1600°C. Zou added 5 vol% WC, which resulted in a room-temperature strength of 605 MPa that steadily increased to 675 MPa at 1600°C. Interestingly, the composition ZrB 2 -20SiC-5ZrC (not shown) exhibited  a  room-temperature  strength  of  1100 MPa, only slightly decreasing to 1020 MPa at 1000°C. Increasing temperatures resulted in a steep decrease in strength to 320 MPa at 1600°C. Grigoriev [15] measured the strength of  a  ZrB 2 -19 vol%  SiC  composite  in  air  (not  shown),  reporting  a  room-temperature strength  of  500  MPa  that  was  maintained  up  to  1200°C,  decreasing  to  430  MPa  at 1400°C. Not only do the room-temperature strengths vary widely for ZrB 2 -SiC, but the change in strength with temperature also varies.

A B S T R A C T
Carbon fibre reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) matrix composites were fabricated coupling water-based powder slurry infiltration, Polymer Infiltration  and Pyrolysis  (PIP)  and  Hot  Pressing (HP)  techniques. This study aims to identify the best sequence of consolidation techniques to better integrate the carbon fibre cloths into an ultra-refractory sintered ceramic matrix of ZrB2-SiC. Infiltrated preforms with UHTC powder slurry were densified via: a) a pre-sintering step by HP followed by two PIP cycles with polycarbosilane, and vice versa, b) two PIP cycles followed by a cycle of HP. Flexural strengths at room temperature and at 1500 ◦ C (167 MPa and 592 MPa, respectively) were found to be significantly higher for composites obtained by the second route, suggesting that sintering of polymer-derived SiC during HP improves the structural properties of Cf/ZrB2SiC  composites. This  work  presents  an effective  method  for  UHTCMC  manufacturing in  a  shorter time  than traditional PIP process.
possessing a good strength even at 2100 ◦ C [13,14], a good fracture toughness (e.g. > 15 MPa m 1/2 ) [13,15,16], an excellent thermal shock resistance ( > 1500 ◦ C) [13], and an excellent ablation resistance above 1600 ◦ C [11,16 -21]. However, the choice of the manufacturing process remains  the  major  concern  for  these  materials.  Selection  of  the  best route is dictated by the need of fast processing/cost-effectiveness combined with quality of performance.

A B S T R A C T
Thermal shock resistance of continuous carbon /uniFB01 ber reinforced ZrC based ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composite (C/C-ZrC) from ultra high temperatures (particularly > 1500 °C) to the room temperature was evaluated using a novel self-developed equipment with high heating-cooling rates in controllable atmosphere. Residual strength and mass variation of the as-prepared composite under di /uniFB00 erent thermal shock cycles and temperatures were tested to characterize the thermal shock resistance. Flexural strength of the composite slightly decreases initially without obvious weight loss below 1300 °C, while it decreases by relatively high values at 1300 -1900 °C and then sharply decreases over 1900 °C with obvious increase of weight loss. Microstructure of the thermally shocked specimens was examined to reveal the thermal shock damage. Matrix cracking, interfacial debonding and matrix pores were clearly observed, which were the main reasons for the strength degradation of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent cycles and temperatures.
the composites subjected to thermal shock. Thermal stresses can be unavoidably introduced into the composites as well due to the thermal expansion mismatch between the carbon /uniFB01 ber and ceramic matrix. Additionally, the /uniFB01 ber or matrix types, interfacial structure and fabrication processes, also have a great in /uniFB02 uence on thermal shock resistance of ceramic matrix composites [6 -10]. Therefore, thermal cycling damage to the C/C-ZrC composite under severe thermal shock conditions must be well understood before its actual applications in these environments.

thermal stability, making it suitable for use at temperatures of 1600 ◦ C. 2-4 The ZrB2's impressive resistance to high-temperature structural degradation is attributed to the in situ formation of a thermally stable ZrO 2 -rich oxide scale, which provides additional thermal insulation and enhances its overall performance. 4-6 Incorporating silicon carbide (SiC) as reinforcement into the ZrB 2 matrix enhances its mechanical properties, oxidation, and ablation resistance. 6,7 Furthermore, the SiC reinforcement also inhibits ZrB2 grain growth during sintering, leading to enhanced densification. The composites with 20 vol% SiC reinforcement are found to offer the best combination of mechanical and thermophysical properties, along with high ablation resistance at elevated temperatures. 7-9
Nevertheless, both monolithic ZrB 2 and ZrB2-SiC composites exhibit brittle catastrophic failure behavior typical of ceramics, significantly limiting their structural applications. 10

A B S T R A C T
The ZrB2 is widely recognized as an ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) due to its high melting point, high thermal conductivity, and superior  thermal  stability,  making  it  suitable  for  application  at  temperatures ≥ 1400 ◦ C [1 -3]. The ZrB2  exhibits a very remarkable resistance to high-temperature structural degradation, thanks to the in-situ formation of a thermally stable ZrO2-rich oxide scale, further enhancing its overall performance by providing thermal insulation [4 -8]. Furthermore, the addition of SiC as reinforcement in the ZrB2 matrix not only improves its strength and fracture toughness, but also its ability to withstand oxidation and ablation [5,7]. Another advantage of the SiC being present as reinforcement is its ability to impede the growth of ZrB2 grains during the sintering process, resulting in superior densification, leading  to  enhanced  mechanical  properties.  It  has  been  conclusively demonstrated  that  among  the  ZrB2-based  composites  with  different proportions of SiC, the one containing 20 vol % SiC as reinforcement exhibits the most favourable combination of mechanical and thermo-physical properties, along with high resistance to ablation at elevated  temperatures  [9,10].  However,  both  monolithic  ZrB2 and ZrB2 -SiC composites show the same type of brittle catastrophic failure Design  and  optimization  of  the  process  for  fabricating  ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) with continuous carbon fibre (Cf) as a reinforcement has recently garnered significant interest among the researchers globally [12]. The strong interest in this area of research stems from the remarkable improvement in fracture toughness and thermal shock resistance, along with the observation of damage tolerant behaviour in the continuous Cf  reinforced UHTCMCs. Due to the combination of continuous carbon fibre and UHTC matrix, these composites attain the thermal and mechanical properties desirable for  use  in  structural  components  of  hypersonic  vehicles,

1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.

Adding ZrC can be a good trick for compensating such a drop in hardness and flexural strength. Adding ZrC to ZrB2-SiC composites, individually, has also been investigated by several researchers [21-25]. Table 1 summarizes the average values of relative density and mechanical properties for some of ZrB2-based ceramics containing SiC, ZrC and/or Cf additives.

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

Directionally Solidified Ceramic Eutectics for High-Temperature Applications
where K C stands  for  the  fracture  toughness  (Llorca  &  Orera,  2006).  Thus  the  improvements in strength of DSE ceramics are associated with an increase of the toughness or a reduction of the defect size, which is normally related to the characteristic dimension of the microstructure. At the same time the crack deflection is a well-known toughening mechanism in ceramic composites with columnar or lamellar structures. For instance, crack deflection in fiber-reinforced ceramics or laminated ceramics is achieved through weak fiber-matrix interfaces or by inserting layers with very low toughness, respectively (Pardo, Peña, Merino, Cases, Larrea, & Orera, 2002). In case of DSE ceramics, the fracture toughness of single phase ceramic layers is not so high and it allows to realise crack deflection toughening mechanism. On the other hand, the thermal residual stresses induced by a thermal expansion mismatch between the phases in DSE ceramics could be used to increase the toughness by enhancing the crack deflection (Llorca & Orera, 2006).
Reduction in the domain thickness can also be achieved by adding a third phase to form a ternary eutectic composite. For example, ternary eutectic Al 2 O3 -YAGYSZ showed higher room temperature strength than binary Al 2 O3 -YAG grown at the same rate (Lee, Yoshikawa, Fukuda, & Waku, 2001; Waku, Sakata, Mitani, Shimizu, & Hasebe, 2002). This indicates that the defect size for the Al 2 O3 -YAG binary eutectic was controlled by the lamella thickness. At present the maximum flexural strength (4.6 GPa) was achieved for DSE Al 2 O3 -YAG-YSZ (Oliete, Peña, Larrea, Orera, Llorca, Pastor, Martin, & Segurado, 2007). This is approximately four times higher than the best results measured in bulk non-oxide ceramics and twice

3.1. SiC whisker reinforcement
Another emerging trend regarding whisker reinforced UHTCMCs is laminated  architectures.  Specifically,  set  of  recent  studies  [119,120] have  investigated  the  effect  of  having  porous  UHTC -SiCw  interfaces between  monolithic  UHTC  layers.  Weak  interfaces  such  as  BN  and graphite  have  previously  been  applied  to  induce  crack  deflection  in these lamented structures [121,122]. However, both BN and graphite are prone to oxidation at lower than desirable temperatures ( ≤ 800 ◦ C). Alternatively, a dense SiC layer has the potential to manifest as an undesirably strong interface. A layer composed of a UHTC ceramic with a high  volume  fraction  of  whiskers  could  instead  produce  a  relatively porous  interface  that  can  concomitantly  resist  oxidation  and  induce toughening mechanisms. This was achieved using whiskers of about 18 μ m length and 1.5 μ m diameter. The fabricated architecture is shown in Fig. 5a, where crack deflection and branching are apparent at the SiCw-UHTC  interface.  Fig.  5b  and  c  further  illustrate  how  microscale whisker  pullout  and  bridging  can  enforce  the  macroscale  crack  deflections. The reported flexure strengths were similar to values reported M. Ammendola et al.

3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
Other recent investigations have proposed functionally graded materials (FGM) for embedding SiCf  in UHTC matrices. Silvestroni et al. [147] synthesized ZrB2-based composites where the fiber volume fraction  increases  as  a  function  of  the  material  depth,  where  the  thin, outermost layer of the material contains the lowest fiber fraction. In principle, this outermost skin should possess a high resistance against oxidation and ablation, and it serves to protect the inner layers of the FGM  that  are  embedded  with  higher  volume  fractions  of  SiCf,  The described  architecture  merited  a  composite  with  reasonable  fracture toughness values (~6 MPa m ½ ) while maintaining low overall densities; this  suggested  a  potential  for  lightweight  component  design  using fiber-reinforced FGMs. Furthermore, load-displacements curves (Fig.  9b)  indicated  failure  behavior  that  was  not  completely  brittle, based  on  the  tailed  shape  at  higher  displacements.  The  subsequent investigation by Stricker et al. [148] further developed the ZrB2  FGM approach by refining the buffer layer architecture. In this design, the outermost  layer  of  the  composite  contained  a  MoSi2 additive  for enhanced oxidation protection, followed by a ' buffer layer ' that mixed ZrB2 with Si3N4, SiCN, or SiHfBCN. The buffer layer served to obstruct diffusion of Mo species from the outer skin to the inner composite, where it could detrimentally react with SiCf during sintering.
In terms of mechanical properties, SiCf-reinforced composites have reported  advantages  over  monolithic  ceramics,  specifically  in  higher fracture toughness values and retained flexural strength up to 1200 ◦ C. Nevertheless, reaction between the fibers and the matrix phase during sintering  remains  an  ongoing  issue,  which  severely  limits  effective toughening  by  fiber  pullout.  Next  generation  SiC  fibers  with  higher purities  have  the  potential  to  resist  matrix  interaction  during  high temperature pressing, but these materials have not yet been explored as reinforcements for UHTCMCs [149].

3.3. Correlating interfaces with the mechanical properties
In summary, a correlation of the microstructural features with enhancement in the mechanical properties is established for ZrB2-SiCCNT composites and schematically presented in Fig. 7. A comparison of the mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites (see Fig. 7 a ), unequivocally, establishes the synergy of SiC and CNT reinforcement in enhancing the properties of ZrB2. The enhancement in the fracture strength, fracture toughness, elastic modulus and reinforcement factor was found to synergistically increase with both SiC and CNT addition (in Z20S10C when compared with monolithic ZrB2). Further, based on the microstructural evolution and enhancement in the fracture toughness, the toughening mechanism with SiC and CNT reinforcement has been delineated, schematically shown in Fig. 7 b . It was found that the strong interfacing between the reinforcement and the matrix, and phenomenon like crack de /uniFB02 ection, branching and bridging (shown in Figs. 3, 5 and 6) attributes in enhancing the strength and toughness of ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites. The result revealed that the enhanced mechanical performance of Z20S10C get an edge over other ZrB2based composites making it a suitable candidate structural material for high damage tolerance.

3.3. Mechanical properties
Fig. 8 shows the variation of hardness of ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites with the reinforcement content. The average hardness of SPS sintered, with relative density of ∼ 85%, was found to be 16.64 GPa in this investigation. The average hardness of the ZrB2-SiC composites increased with increasing SiC reinforcement content, reaching ∼ 22.71 GPa for composites with 40% SiC. The higher hardness for the ZrB2-SiC composites compared to ZrB2 seems to be due to combination of effects such as higher relative density and presence of high volume fraction of harder reinforcement (SiC) particles. As all the ZrB2-SiC composites (10-40%
Fig. 9. Flexural strength of (a) ZrB2-SiC, and (b) ZrB2-CNT composites as a function of reinforcement content.

3.3. Mechanical properties
sintered in this investigation seems primarily due to lower relative density ( ∼ 85%). While the SiC reinforcement resulted in the improvement in fracture toughness of ZrB2, the average fracture toughness values of all the ZrB2-SiC composites remained in the range of 2-2.7 MPa m 1/2 . Among ZrB2-SiC composites, the composites reinforced with 20% SiC exhibited highest fracture of 2.66 ± 0.34 MPa m 1/2 . For ZrB2-CNT composites, the fracture toughness also increased with increasing CNT reinforcement content and was in the range of 1.5-3.5 MPa m 1/2 . The ZrB2-CNT composites reinforced with 6% CNTs exhibited highest fracture toughness of 3.53 ± 0.47 MPa m 1/2 . Fig. 11 shows the morphologies of indentation cracks propagated through the SPS sintered ZrB2, ZrB2-SiC, and ZrB2-CNT composites. In ZrB2 samples, the crack front was relatively straight without significant bending or deflection (Fig. 11(a)). However, the crack seems to propagate along boundaries of smaller grains (1-2 𝛍 m). The fracture surface of ZrB2 samples indicated predominantly intergranular fracture features with distributed porosity (Fig. 3(a)). For ZrB2-SiC composites, the indentation crack interacts with the reinforced SiC particles causing shear of the finer particles and crack getting deflected at the coarser particles. The fracture surface of the ZrB2-SiC composites also shows predominantly intergranular fracture features with distributed depressions due to grain pull-outs (Fig. 3(b-c)). The relatively higher fracture toughness of these ZrB2-SiC composites compared to ZrB2 samples seems to be due to better densification and additional toughening mechanisms (SiC grain pull-outs and crack deflection).

3. Results and discussion
The bulk density of the ZrB2-20vol.% SiCW composites hotpressed at 2000 ◦ C was 5.44g/cm 3 , which corresponds to 98.6% of the theoretical density estimated with the rule of mixture. The flexural strength and fracture toughness of ZrB2-SiCW composites at room temperature are 510 ± 25MPa and 4.05 ± 0.20MPam 1/2 , respectively. However, the flexural strength and the fracture toughnessofSiCwhisker-reinforcedZrB2 werenotsignificantlyincreased compared with those without reinforcements. The values of flexural strength and the fracture toughness are similar to those of SiC particle reinforced ZrB2 composites. Amazingly, SiC whiskers have been degenerated into particles (Fig. 2). Furthermore, the microstructure of the SiC whisker reinforced ZrB2 composite is very similar to that of the SiC particle reinforced (not shown).

Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix = Zhi Wang, Dalian University of Technology, China. Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix = Zhanjun Wu, Dalian University of Technology, China
The crystal structure, synthesis, and densification of zirconium diboride (ZrB2) are summarized in detail. In this chapter, ZrB2-ZrC-SiC ceramic was synthesized by reactive hot pressing a mixture of Zr, B4C, and Si powders. The thermal shock resistance of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was estimated by the water-quenching method and was significantly greater than that of a ZrB2-15vol.% SiC ceramic. The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was carried out in static air at constant temperatures of 1000±15, 1200±15, and 1400±15 ºC for different amounts of time at each temperature. The mechanism of strength increase for the oxidized specimen indicated that the strength increased with the reaction rate, which was related to the rate of change in volume induced by reaction, initial crack geometry, elastic modulus, and surface free energy. The formation of oxide layers resulted in (I) repair of surface flaws, (II) increase in flexural strength, (III) appearance of a compressive stress zone beneath the surface oxide layers, (IV) decrease in thermal stress, and (V) consumption of thermal stress. These five aspects were favorable to the improvement of the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic. The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was carried out in static air at 1600±15 ºC. In the different oxidation stages, quantitative models were proposed for predicting oxidation kinetics.

3.2. Microstructure of the as-sintered material
As example, the typical microstructure (cross-section) of as-prepared C/ZrB2-SiC composites (BAR and UBR samples) is shown in Fig. 2.
The low magnification micrographs (Fig. 2a,b) evidence the layered structure  in  a  0/90 ◦ and  0/0 ◦ configurations  with  homogeneously distributed carbon fibers in a nearly fully dense ZrB2/SiC matrix with no delamination. The volumetric fiber content amounted to about 50 vol% as ascertained by image analysis, while the matrix consisted of about 45 vol% ZrB2  and 5 vol% SiC (Fig. 2c). Interlocking among fibers, SiC and ZrB2 particles is well highlighted in Fig. 2d,e. During the sintering treatment, the high chemical compatibility of ZrB2  with carbon led to enhanced reactivity between fiber and matrix, resulting in strong interfaces.  The  proposed  thermal  treatment  at  temperature  close  to 2000 ◦ C and pressure of tens of MPa for few minutes time followed by a rapid  cooling  guaranteed  a  strong  fiber/matrix  interface  without causing critical fiber damage.
As  can  be  seen  in  high  magnification  micrograph  in  Fig.  2f,  the presence  of  SiC  particles  (dark  grey  regions)  at  grain  boundaries inhibited  the  excessive  grain  growth  of  bright  contrast  ZrB2 (small variations in color are due to the different grain orientations) [18,19]. As a result, the microstructure was very fine-grained, with a mean ZrB2 grain size of 2.2 µ m. Similar microstructure was found for UD sample.

1. Introduction
