3.3. Plasma arc jet exposure
Further,  the  cross-sectional  SEM  of  samples  was  conducted  to quantify the thickness of the oxide film formation on the sample surface due to the melting of the Ta2O5  phase, see Fig. 9c and d. The minor cracking in the 50HO-50TO sample vs. wide crack opening in the 70HO30TO sample in the unaffected region elicits that the intense plasma exposure was enough to create thermal stresses, analogous to the strain analysis in Table 1. The thickness of melted and then resolidified oxide film is 210 nm and 297 nm in 50HO-50TO and 70HO-30TO samples. Although there is no significant change in the thickness of melted oxide in both the samples, there are significantly more apparent melted regions in the 70HO-30TO sample. Hence, the 50HO-50TO sample has better  thermal  stability  than  the  70HO-30TO  sample.  In  terms  of improving the oxidation resistance of solid-solution binary UHTCs or MC-UHTCs, it can be anticipated that a composition with equal or higher content of TaC will have a higher probability of Hf6Ta2O17  phase formation during plasma exposure. This complex mixed oxide phase act as a protective layer that suppresses further oxidation. Also, the protective layer in UHTC components is formed due to the preferential oxidation of various  components  at  service  temperature  [16].  The  present  study shows that the complex mixed oxide formation during service ( > 2500 ◦ C) of solid-solution binary UHTCs and MC-UHTCs improves its oxidation resistance due to the thermal stability and mechanical integrity of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase. UHTC composition, thus designed to form a protective and thermally stable Hf6Ta2O17 phase under extreme conditions, will provide new directions to the materials for reusable hypersonic applications.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have shown aspiration to overcome challenges in the thermal protection system (TPS) by designing new materials referred to as multi-component UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) in the compositional space. MC-UHTCs have shown remarkable improvement in oxidation resistance due to the formation of the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure during plasma exposure. Herein, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure is synthesized via a solid-state reaction between HfO2 and Ta2O5 powder mixtures during spark plasma sintering (SPS). The compositions chosen are 50 vol% of HfO2 -50 vol% of Ta2O5 (50HO-50TO) and 70 vol% of HfO2 -30 vol% of Ta2O5 (70HO-30TO). The phase quantification via Rietveld analysis showed Hf6Ta2O17 as a principal phase with some residual Ta2O5  phase in both the samples. The high-temperature thermal stability of the samples was evaluated using high-velocity plasma jet exposure for up to 3 min. 50HO-50TO was able to withstand the intense plasma condition, which is attributed to the higher content of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase (~84%) and lower strain in the Ta2O5 phase. The augmentation in the Hf6Ta2O17 phase to 94.7% (in 50HO-50TO) post plasma exposure has been attributed to the invariant transformation from a liquid state to Hf6Ta2O17 at temperatures > 2500 ◦ C during testing. The mechanical integrity is elucidated from the insignificant change in the hardness ~13.3 GPa before and 11.2 GPa after plasma exposure of the 50HO-50TO sample. As a result, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure ' s thermo-mechanical stability suggests developing novel oxidation-resistant MC-UHTCs in compositional space for reusable space vehicle applications.

2.3. Characterization of plasma jet exposed MC-UHTCs

[10] showed oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-graphene nanoplatelets (1 to 5 vol%) (GNP) composites processed at temperature of 1850 ◦ C under high temperature plasma flow of 30 kW power for 60 s and showed that addition of GNP suppresses formation of oxide layer up to 60%. However, high temperature thermal conductivity for TaC based composites is not available in literature, which is one of the important attributes to survive high temperature exposure.
Improvement of fracture toughness with SiC and CNT addition in TaC-based UHTCs is demonstrated elsewhere [39]. Hence, the scope of the present work is to develop an understanding of the oxidation mechanisms of TaC-SiC-CNT composites to predict the lifetime performance of UHTCs under operational conditions. Oxidation behaviour is analysed in terms of the oxide layer formation during testing. To supplement plasma arc jet testing, thermogravimetric analysis is carried out for 30 min. at 1500 ◦ C in oxygen environment. Thermal conductivity of these materials have been measured using laser flash diffusivity technique and, accordingly, correlation of the heat transfer mechanism during plasma arc jet testing is established. An attempt has been made to associate the synergistic effect of SiC and CNT reinforcement on the performance of TaC-based UHTCs through these investigations.

3.3. Surface oxidation state
Table 3 summarizes the Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, Hf4f, O1s, and C1s peaks from XPS spectra collected for the plasma exposed binary, ternary, and quaternary UHTCs along with the literature peaks for carbides and oxides. The relative intensity of elements ' peaks in XPS spectra can provide insight into oxidation thermodynamic and kinetic theories. This can be well  understood by comparing the relative intensities of various elements in a particular sample. The Gibbs free energy of oxide formation for Group V (Ta,Nb) is lower than that of Group IV (Hf,Ti), other things being equal [28]. Hence, the preferential oxide growth of Ti and Hf are more favorable once the oxidation process is activated [26], as observed from the XRD analysis (see Table 2). The oxidation in MC-UHTCs is led by the oxygen diffusion inwards and the metal cation outwards with time. This outward diffusion of metal cation depends on the size of the A. Nisar et al.

3.3. Plasma arc jet exposure

Post-exposure of the samples showed that 50HO-50TO survived the plasma exposure even up to 3 min, with some apparent cracks on the sample surface and a slight change in sample color (white) due to the removal of diffused C, see Fig. 7a. However, the 70HO-30TO sample couldn ' t sustain the thermal shock associated with plasma exposure and broke into two pieces. The color of the 70HO-30TO sample changed significantly to white, indicating some melting of the oxide phase. The higher thermal stability of the 50HO-50TO sample has been attributed to the higher content of Hf6Ta2O17  phase formation during SPS. This indicates that the oxide composition is important in providing oxidation resistance  to  UHTCs.  The  result  obtained  elucidates  the  remarkable improvement in the solid-solution phase of (Ta,Hf)C with composition 50 vol% TaC-50 vol% HfC (up to 65%) over other compositions i.e. HfC, 20 vol%HfC-80 vol%TaC, 80 vol%HfC-20 vol%TaC, and TaC [17].

1.1. Expected oxidation behavior of group IV compounds with group V and VI additions
Group V additions to group IV ceramics in various forms, mainly as Ta compounds, have resulted in some limited improvement in oxidation behavior, whether through liquid phase assisted sintering of the group IV oxides, the doping of group IV oxides with higher valence ions to limit oxygen transport through oxygen vacancies [13], or the formation of ordered compounds such as A6B2O17 (A = Hf, Zr; B = Ta, Nb) [13,22] (Fig. 1(a)). On the other hand, liquid oxide formation at more aggressive conditions  and  ultra-high  temperatures  has  been  shown  to  be  detrimental to the oxidation behavior of UHTCs. Results discussed in previous work [9] for the group IV + V combination shows that the oxidation behavior of the solid solution closely matches the design considerations [5] from the constituent elements, i.e., the formation of group V oxides, for example, promoted the formation of oxides with low melting temperatures  that  resulted  in  a  decrease  in  oxidation  resistance  of  the material.

A B S T R A C T
High entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics (HE-UHTCs) have garnered intense research interest due to the potential for optimized oxidation and mechanical properties for extreme environment applications. HE-UHTCs are  expected to oxidize according to the thermodynamic favorability of their respective oxidation reactions, which varies according to the periodic grouping. Based on this, the oxidation resistance of equimolar (metalsbasis) group IV + V (HfZrTiTaNb), group IV + V + VI (HfZrTiTaMo) and group IV + VI (HfZrTiMoW) carbides and borides were evaluated at 1700 ◦ C in 1 mol% O2  for 5 min and compared. Group IV elements oxidized preferentially in all three compositions. Group V element-containing carbides exhibited the lowest oxidation resistance, attributed to the formation of intergranular liquid oxides. (HfZrTiMoW)C exhibited the best resistance among the carbides. The diborides exhibited similar material consumption, reinforcing the hypothesis that the oxidation behavior under these conditions is controlled by the presence of boria. These findings provide direction for HE-UHTC composition design for oxidation resistance.

Additionally, CrB2 additions were found to be less effective than Ta or Nb-based additions in improving oxidation resistance [6]. Vanadium oxides have low melting temperatures 5 and form low-temperature melting eutectics with the oxides of the other elements under consideration, e.g. a eutectic in the ZrO2 -V2O5 is noted at 665 ◦ C [7], It is important to consider whether the thermodynamic considerations previously discussed [5,8] can be extended to compositions with group VI additions and determine whether they will oxidize to form volatile products. Therefore, a systematic study for the design of an oxidation resistant HE-UHTC, was considered as follows:

4. Conclusions
Oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-SiC-CNT composites was evaluated through plasma arc jet exposure under heat flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 . Oxidation behaviour is characterised in terms of oxide layer formation, its thickness and residual stresses. After exposure to harsh aerothermal conditions, the structural integrity of these UHTC composites were measured in terms of critical energy release rate ( G IC ) which increases up to 2.5 times ( ∼ 58 to 139 Jm -2 ) with synergistic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in TaC. The protective SiO2 formation with SiC reinforcement in TaC suggested an extension of stability domain which resulted in an improved oxidation resistance. It is elicited that CNT addition improves oxidation resistance of TaC by grain sealing mechanism. Raman spectroscopic analysis elicited the presence of carbonaceous material even after plasma exposure, further supplemented by TEM analysis of T15S15C. In addition, enhanced thermal conductivity with temperature for all composites provides an indication of improved oxidation resistance via heat dissipation. Thermal studies up to 1500 ◦ C have confirmed that the temperature of oxide formation shifts towards higher temperature (from 735 ◦ C to 901 ◦ C) and decrease in the enthalpy of oxide formation (from 4.5 to 3.5 kJ/g) with SiC and CNT reinforcement. Such an observation insinuates synergetic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in enhancing the thermal stability and oxidation resistance of TaC-based UHTC composites.

3.2. Phase quantification of surface oxide layer
In the view of kinetic theory, multi-element oxide formation is highly favorable due to the comparable diffusivity of Group IV and V oxides [39].  Based  on  the  thermal  stability  of  formed  oxides,  preferential oxidation  occurs  governing  the  quantity  of  the  multi-element  oxides formed [26,28]. Among all refractory elements, oxides from group IV elements (Hf, Ti) owing to the highest melting point (Ta2O5 ~ 1872 ◦ C; Nb2O5  ~ 1915 ◦ C;  TiO2  ~ 1843 ◦ C;  HfO2  and other mixed complex oxides > 2400 ◦ C  [2,7,29])  are  most  thermodynamically  favored. Hence, ternary UHTC has oxide scale rich in Hf while quaternary rich in Ti (see Table 2). To further supplement the result obtained from XRD analysis,  core-level  spectroscopy  using  the  XPS  technique  has  been carried out.
